name: POMZ-2 anti-personnel mine
description:
POMZ ( Protivopekhotnaya Oskolochnaya Mina Zagrazhdeniya - Anti-personnel Fragmentation Defence Mine) is a series of Soviet stake mounted anti-personnel fragmentation mine, including POMZ, POMZ-2 and POMZ-2M. First version of POMZ was issued in late 1930s. Copies of POMZ series mines were produced in China (Type 58 and Type 59), Yugoslavia (PMR-1 and PMR-2A), Czechoslovakia (PP Mi-SK), Myanmar (MM-1), Vietnam (MBV-78A1) and Cuba (PMFH-1 and PMFH-2).

The POMZ-2 anti-personnel mine and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Podewils-Lindner Rifle
description:
The Podewils-Lindner Rifle was part of the equipment of the Royal Bavarian Army in the mid-19th century in its various versions. It was developed by Philipp von Podewils, the director of the Royal Bavarian Rifle Factory in Amberg, to replace the M-1842 musket. Originally a muzzle-loader, the rifle was converted to a breech-loader in 1867; the so-called Lindner bolt system was used for this purpose. Following a proposal submitted by Eduard Lindner to the Bavarian Crown, a breech was installed that allowed paper cartridges to be loaded from the rear and fired with the existing percussion lock. There were three different models, which differed only in their overall and barrel lengths.

In Bavaria, the muzzle-loading rifle did not become the army's standard weapon as it did in Prussia. Only through experience gained in various battles did it become clear in Bavaria that the era of the muzzle-loader had ended. Although the Bavarian Army had developed the Podewils M58 rifle, which compared very favorably in terms of accuracy and penetrating power, it fell behind the Prussian breech-loading models as a muzzle-loader in the war of 1866. Therefore, immediately after the war, a modification of the rifle to a breech-loader was requested and finally approved by King Ludwig II on September 11, 1866. In 1867, the muzzle-loaders were successfully replaced with the new breech-loading model M/58/67, and by the end of the year, the Bavarian Army had some 110, 000 new rifles at its disposal. These also constituted the main weapon of the Bavarian infantry in the war against France, during which it received its nickname: Kaffeemühle (coffee grinder).

specs.Length: 52. 36 inches (133 cm; Muster I), 51. 18 inches (130 cm; Muster II), 48. 03 inches (122 cm; Muster III)
specs.Barrel length: 37 inches (94 cm; Muster I & II), 32. 92 inches (83. 6 cm; Muster II)

name: Pontiac M39
description:
The Pontiac M39 is a 20mm revolver cannon developed for service in the US Air Force in the late-1940's. Like the British ADEN cannon and French DEFA cannon, it is based on the German MG213 revolver cannon which never entered service. The M39 was the standard gun armament for all USAF fighters until the M61 Vulcan came along, which fired the same round. Despite both being 20mm and introduced into US service around the same time, ammunition for the Navy's Colt Mk 12 cannon is not interchangeable.

The Pontiac M39 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PP-2000
description:
The PP-2000 is a Russian submachine gun developed by the KBP Instrument Design Bureau of Tula, Russia. Created in 2001, it was first shown to the public at a weapons trade show in 2004. It was launched into mass production in 2006 and is currently being fielded in limited numbers with law enforcement units in Russia. It is designed specifically to use Russian 7N21 and 7N31 overpressure 9x19mm ammunition, with the intent being to produce armour-piercing capability to compete in the vaguely-defined PDW market. The PP-2000 can also fire conventional 9x19mm Parabellum rounds.

The PP-2000 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Length: 21. 9 in (55. 5 cm) (stock extended) / 13. 4 in (34 cm) (stock folded)

name: Povzbroj PS-97 Arrow
description:
The Povzbroj PS-97 is a Slovak pistol with short-lived production, based on the CZ-75. The Slovak army ordered 32, 000 pistols, but due to various difficulties, only 1, 600 were produced before the Povzbroj firm finally went bankrupt in 2001. There were attempts to continue development, but these were ultimately unsuccessful, with the PS-97 losing out in trials to the Grand Power K100.

The Povzbroj PS-97 Arrow and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PP-90
description:
The PP-90 is an attempt to copy the concept (and probably some design ideas) of the American prototype Ares FMG (Folding Machine Gun), developed in early 1990s by KBP Instrument Design Bureau in Tula. Both the Ares FMG and its PP-90 clone were intended for concealed carry, and can be folded down to a relatively compact, plain looking metal box. When needed, these guns were to be unfolded into a ready-to-fire position in 3 - 4 seconds. Other weapons of similar concept include the UC-9 and Magpul FMG-9.

The PP-90 is a full-auto only weapon. The PP-90M is its select-fire variant. A 9x19mm Parabellum variant was also introduced later, designated the PP-92. Decpite produced from early 1990s, PP-90 and PP-90M were officially adopted for Russian law enforcement only in December 1996. Information about the number of pieces produced varies enormously: from 2, 000 to 60, 000(! ). They were also exported to Kazakhstan and Mongolia.

name: PP-91 Kedr
description:
The PP-91 Kedr (An acronym for K onstruktsiya Ye vgeniya Dr agunova, Russian: Конструкция Евгения Драгунова, lit. "Design by Yevgeniy Dragunov", and also literally meaning "Cedar" in Russian) is a Russian select-fire submachine gun. Although it was initially designed in 1969-1972 by Yevgeny Dragunov (as the PP-71 ), it wasn't issued then as it was intended to replace them with AKS-74U, which it failed to. The project was "rediscovered" in 1991 by order of the USSR Ministry of Internal Affairs (with the introduction of a number of changes, primarily the transfer of loading the magazine from the pistol grip to a more "traditional" position in front of the trigger group), with the start of first production in 1992 and mass production since 1993.

Variants of the Kedr include the improved PP-9 Klin, the suppressed PP-91-01 Kedr-B, the 9x19mm PP-919 Kedr-2 and the non-lethal PDT-9T "Yesaul" and "Yesaul-3" (full-auto "Yesaul-2" variant was not introduced due to restrictions of Russian legislation).

name: PP-90M1
description: The PP-90M1 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PPD-44
description:
The PPD-44 (to some extent a conventional name) was developed by gunsmith Vasiliy Dolganov for partisan forces operating in Belarus. It was a simple, but combat-ready weapon, in the spirit of the Sten. It is possible to use broken weapons, as well as scrap metal, in its manufacture. It was likely produced in double-digit quantities at maximum.

The PPD-44 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PP-93
description:
The PP-93 is a Russian submachine gun developed in 1993 by the KBP Instrument Design Bureau for use by security and law enforcement units.

The PP-93 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PROM-1 anti-personnel mine
description:
PROM-1 is a Yugoslavian bounding anti-personnel mine, based on WW2 German S-Mine.

The PROM-1 anti-personnel mine and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PPS Submachine Gun
description:
The PPS submachine gun (Russian: Пистолет-пулемёт Судаева; Pistolet Pulemyot Sudayeva ) is a Soviet submachine gun designed by Alexey Sudayev, based on an experimental submachine gun designed by Lieutenant Bezruchko-Vysotsky. The PPS was developed in 1942, in response to the Red Army's call for a submachine gun that was lighter, more compact, and (most importantly) cheaper than the PPSh-41. While prototypes were developed throughout the year, the first significant production of the PPS occurred during the Siege of Leningrad within the city itself. The initial variant, dubbed the PPS-42, saw around 45, 000 units produced from 1942 to 1943. The PPS-42 can be distinguished by the separate shell ejector, 20 mm longer barrel and a barrel shroud separated from the receiver.

The PPS-42 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Length: 32. 7 in (83 cm) stock extended / 24. 4 in (62 cm) stock folded

name: Protector Palm Pistol
description:
The Protector Palm Pistol, sometimes referred to as the "Chicago Protector" or "Minneapolis Protector, " is a small, seven shot turret revolver developed in the late 19th century.

The Protector Palm Pistol and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PWS MK1 rifle series
description:
The Primary Weapons Systems MK1 Series Rifles are modern AR-15 -pattern rifles using an AK -style long stroke piston system, stated by the manufacturer to be meant to improve reliability. Each rifle in the MK1 family is available as a complete rifle or as a complete upper receiver for existing M4/M16-style lowers.

The original MK1 was available in. 223 Wylde (a specific chamber design meant for better compatibility with both 5. 56x45mm NATO and. 223 Remington), and was typically seen in an 11. 5" SBR configuration.

name: PSS Silent Pistol
description:
The Russian PSS ( Pistolet Spetsialnyj Samozaryadnyj - special self-loading pistol, GRAU index 6P28) is a pistol using the unique 7. 62x42mm noiseless cartridge, developed in 1980. A Soviet-era Black Ops weapon, it was used by Russian secret police or special forces.

Contrary to what might be expected, the relatively long cartridge length for this pistol is not largely filled with smokeless powder (as is the case for most conventional pistol cartridges), but is instead dedicated to a unique piston system that, when the cartridge is triggered, delivers enough force to fire the bullet to an effective range of 25 m, afterwards sealing the cartridge neck to prevent the escape of flash, smoke, or noise. This unique system is what allows the PSS to achieve virtually noiseless semi-automatic operation without the use of bulky suppressors or slide locks that would hamper its concealability or quick deployment. This same feature, however, is also responsible for the weapon's very short range.

name: PSL
description:
The Romanian PSL (Romanian: Puşcă Semiautomată cu Lunetă, lit. "scoped semi-automatic rifle") sniper rifle was developed in the 1970s as a designated marksman rifle, much like the American M21 and Soviet SVD. The PSL is built using an RPK stamped receiver with the AK long stroke piston, but chambered for 7. 62x54mmR. The Romanians never really used the PSL as a sniper rifle in their armed forces, instead choosing other long range weapons (such as commercial bolt action rifles) for that task.

The PSL is commonly encountered in the U. S. and is often sold as a Dragunov to the uninformed. In the United States, the PSL is also known under the names ascribed by U. S. distributors like ROMAK-3 and PSL-54C (Century Imports), SSG-97 (as called by Interordnance) and the FPK or FPK Dragunov (as sold by Tennessee Guns which are assembled in the U. S with Romanian parts).

name: PSM
description: The PSM and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PTRS-41
description:
The PTRS-41 ( Protivotankovoye samozaryadnoye ruzhyo obraztsa 1941 goda sistemy Simonova, lit. Antitank-self-loading gun pattern 1941, Simonov system) is a Soviet anti-tank rifle developed and used during World War II. Numerous PTRS-41s were captured by Wehrmacht and used by Axis armies, where it's designated as Panzerbüchse 784 (r) or PzB 784 (r).

The PTRS-41 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Capacity: 5-round integral box magazine. Cartridges inserted in a clips.

name: Q The Fix
description: The Q The Fix and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PTRD-41
description:
The PTRD-41 (ProtivoTankovoye Ruzhyo Degtyaryova; Противотанковое однозарядное ружьё системы Дегтярёва образца 1941 года; "Degtyaryov Single Shot Anti-Tank Weapon System Model of 1941") is an anti-tank rifle designed by Vasily Degtyaryov and used by the Red Army during World War II. Is single-shot and take design features from the kb ppanc wz. 35 and Panzerbüchse 39.

The PTRD-41 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: PU-21
description:
The PU-21 is a Soviet machine gun, the final development of the PU series of experimental machine guns tested throughout the late 1970s as a potential replacement for the RPK series in the Soviet Army. The very first version, simply called the PU, heavily resembles the RPK, and features an unusual side-mounted feed tray cover (not unlike the one that would be seen decades later on the FN EVOLYS ). The next version, the PU-2, was a completely original design and shared little in common with older Soviet machine gun designs. It uses long-stroke piston operation, with a rotating bolt, and was rejected for its overly complex feed system. The PU-21 is the final version in this series, and features a greatly simplified feed system, new stock, and redesigned bipod from the PU-2. One of the more notable features of this weapon is its ability to use either belts and or 5. 45mm box magazines designed for the AK-74 and RPK-74, not unlike the FN Minimi / M249 SAW 's ability to accept STANAG magazines.

The PU-21 was trialed throughout 1978, and while performing satisfactory in tests, it displayed no significant improvement over the existing RPK series. The Soviet military abandoned the project that same year, finding the PU-21 too overly complex compared to existing machine guns and opting to continue using the already widespread RPK.

name: QBU-10
description:
The QBU-10 (officially written in Chinese sources as QBU10 ) is a Chinese large-caliber sniper rifle developed from 1999-2010, and formally entered production in 2010. The rifle is also known under the name QBU-09 ( QBU09 ), an apparently unofficial name given to the weapon by an early Chinese magazine publication that reported on the weapon.

The rifle was reportedly developed and manufactured by Sichuan Huaqing Machinery Co. Ltd, a subsidiary of China South Industries Group. The rifle was developed specifically for use in the Chinese People's Liberation Army, and entered service with special units in the PLA in the early 2010s. An additional variant, the NSG-127 is also produced specifically for export use; this version features a differently-designed stock, a two-chamber muzzle brake, and a Picatinny rail spanning most of the upper receiver (with the original version's backup sights being removed to accomodate this).

specs.Caliber (s): 12 gauge (often named as 18. 4mm in Chinese sources)

name: QBU-88
description:
The QBU-88 (officially written in Chinese sources as QBU88, without the hyphen), also known as the "Type 88", is a Chinese bullpup designated marksman rifle in service in the People's Liberation Army. The QBU-88 began development in 1988 and finished development in 1996, and was officially adopted by the PLA in 1997, in time for the Hong Kong handover ceremony. Despite sharing some visual similarities with the QBZ-95 and sharing its ammunition, the QBU-88 is substantially different from the QBZ-95 internally, and their magazines are not interchangeable.

The "QBU" three-letter designation in this weapon's name stands for " q iāng" (Chinese: 枪, "firearm"), " b ùqiāng" (Chinese: 步枪, "rifle") and "j ū jī" (Chinese: 狙击, "sniper").

name: QBZ-95
description:
The Type 95 (Chinese: 95式枪族; "Type 95 firearm family") family of bullpup assault rifles is China's 21st century foray into the world of synthetic bullpup stock assault rifles (like the Steyr AUG, British SA80, and French FAMAS rifles). This series of weapons designs marks the first time the Chinese army (PLA) have diverged from the traditional Kalashnikov styling of rifles and light machine guns.

Despite popular misconception, the Type 95 family is currently primarily designed and manufactured by subsidiaries of China South Industries Group, not Norinco Group (though it was true that the two were the same entity, Norinco (G), during the development of the Type 95). The family includes the QBZ-95 rifle variant, the improved QBZ-95-1 variants, the QBZ-97 variants for export, the QJB-95 light machine gun, and the QBZ-95B carbine variant.

name: QJY-201
description:
The QJY-201 Squad Machine Gun is a 7. 62x51mm NATO Chinese light machine gun.

The QJY-201 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: QBZ-191
description:
The QBZ-191 is a Chinese assault rifle made by Chongqing Jianshe, a subsidiary of China South Industries Group, to gradually replace the QBZ-95 platform used by the People's Liberation Army. It has a conventional rifle layout (in contrast to the bullpup QBZ-95), and has improved ergonomics and ballistic performance. The carbine variant is called the QBZ-192 and the DMR variant (which retains the select fire ability of the 191) is called the QBU-191. A shotgun variant sharing several internal and external parts exists and is called the QBA-221. There also exists a folding survival rifle with unknown designation based on the 191.

The 5. 8x42mm DVC12 (originally named the DBP191) round was specifically designed for the QBZ-191 (it is unknown what the rounds for the carbine and DMR are called). The official suppressor for the rifle is called the VQX191. The export models are called the CS/LR41 (QBZ-191) and CS/LR41A (QBZ-192). For the different caliber export models, see the discussion page.

name: QCW-05
description:
The QCW-05 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

The QCW-05, known as Type 05 Submachine Gun in Chinese, is a Chinese bullpup submachine gun. The weapon is manufactured by China South Industries Group (CSGC), and was designed by Jianshe Industry (Group) Corporation, a subsidiary of CSGC.

name: QJB-201
description:
The QJB-201 Squad Machine Gun is a 5. 8x42mm Chinese light machine gun with manufacture starting in 2016. It has a shorter brother called the QJS-161 Paratrooper Machine Gun.

The QJB-201 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: QJY-88
description:
The QJY-88 (officially written in Chinese sources as QJY88 ) is a Chinese air-cooled, belt fed and gas operated general purpose machine gun developed to replace the Type 67 machine gun. Like the QBZ-95, it uses the 5. 8x42mm rounds, unlike other GPMGs which use heavier rounds such as the 7. 62x51mm NATO or the 7. 62x54mmR. It can be fixed-mounted or it can be hand-wielded.

Like other Chinese small arms, the QJY-88 is commonly misreported as being designed and manufactured by Norinco by internet sources. The weapon is actually designed and manufactured by China's Factory 356, now known as Yunnan Xiyi Industrial Co Ltd, a subsidiary of China South Industries Group.

name: QLG-10A
description: The QLG-10A is a Chinese 35mm underbarrel grenade launcher. It is a variant of the QLG-10 grenade launcher, its designation standing for 轻武器—榴弹—挂, (Pinyin: qīng wǔ qì - liú dàn - guà) meaning "Light weapon - grenade - hung (i. e. underbarrel)". QLG-10 can be mounted to both the QBZ-95 and the improved QBZ-95-1 (which was designed concurrently), while QLG-10A can be mounted on QBZ-95-1 and the carbine QBZ-95B-1. Mounting the QLG-10 requires a special connection mount, while the QLG-10A does not require it.

name: QJZ-89
description: The QJZ-89, also known as "Type 89", is a Chinese heavy machine gun. It was designed by Factory 216 (now Sichuan Huaqing). Its export variant is named W-89.

name: QSB-91
description: The QSB-91 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: QLZ-87
description:
The QLZ-87 and its derivative, the QLB-06, are a pair of unusual repeating grenade launchers developed by China. Developed as a portable weapon for either handheld or crew-served, the QLZ-87 is chambered in the high-velocity Type 87 35mm grenade in a direct-impingement gas-operated weapon using a rotating bolt. Typically, an infantry grenade launcher, such as M203 or China's QLG-10A, uses low-velocity ammo. The Type 87 ammo provides the weapons with a much flatter firing trajectory and a longer range compared to underbarrel launchers.

Though commonly claimed to be a product of Norinco Group, there is no reliable source confirming that the weapon was manufactured by Norinco Group. Some Chinese sources stated that the QLZ-87 was actually developed by a group of Hunanese manufacturers under the lead of Factory 9656 (now known as Hunan Zijiang Machine Co. Ltd. ), predecessors of the Hunan Ordnance Industries Group.

name: QSZ-92
description:
The QSZ-92 is a Chinese pistol, and the current service pistol of the Chinese People's Liberation Army. Though commonly claimed as being designed and manufactured by Norinco Group, Chinese government-affiliated media have primarily indicated that the weapon is designed and manufactured by subsidiaries of the China South Industries Group (CSGC); a 2019 Chinese TV documentary showed that CSGC subsidiary Jianshe is the current primary manufacturer of the QSZ-92.

The Chinese export company Norinco (not to be confused with Norinco Group ) exports two 9mm models of the QSZ-92: the CF98 and the NP42. Both are branded as "Norinco" products, even though they were not manufactured by Norinco.

name: Queen Anne Flintlock Pistol
description:
The Queen Anne (also known as a "turn-off barrel pistol") was an unusual English flintlock pistol in which the lock plate, breech and trigger were forged in one piece, in a similar way to a modern firearm action, so named because they became popular in the reign of Queen Anne (1707-1714), though the mechanism existed since the 1640s.

Most Queen Anne pistols are breech-loaders with turn-off barrels that can be unscrewed from the weapon to insert the ball and powder without ramming or wadding. While this provided a great advantage in increasing reloading speed, Queen Anne pistols were not popular with the military due to their expense and the possibility of dropping the detachable barrel while loading, and were mostly used by civilian shooters for self-defence. An unusually high number of examples were rifled, as the breech-loading nature bypasses the difficulty of loading pre-Minié ball muzzleloading rifles.

name: QW-2
description:
The QW-2 (Chinese: 前卫-2, pinyin: qiánwèi-2; lit. "Vanguard 2"; some online sources also alleges that the QW-2 is exported under the literal "Vanguard 2" name) is a Chinese MANPADS, an improved version of the QW-1 Vanguard which was a Chinese derivative of the Russian SA-16 "Gimlet. "

It was first displayed at the Farnborough Airshow in England in 1998, and is believed to have been adopted by the People's Liberation Army some time between that date and 2002.

name: R.S.C. Mle 1917
description: The Ribeyrolles, Sutter and Chauchat (R. S. C. ) Model 1917, also known as the Fusil Automatique (F. A. ) Model 1917, was a French semi-automatic rifle produced during World War I. Although 85, 000 rifles were produced during the war, it was criticized for being too heavy and too long, and it was accused of being too difficult to maintain in a trench environment. It also needed a special five-round clip. An improved version, the R. S. C. M1918, was shorter, and used the standard five-round clip of the Berthier rifle. This variant proved itself during the Rif War.

name: R9-Arms submachine gun
description:
The R9-Arms is a submachine gun of unknown make and origin. Very little information is truly known about the gun; the only confirmed knowledge regarding it is that there are no known gun manufacturers known as "R9-ARMS CORP. U. S. A. " despite what the gun's markings say, and that the gun has been illegally discovered in nations such as the United States, the Netherlands, and most notably, Croatia, where many copies of the gun have been found. With this and Croatia's prolific history of weapon smuggling in mind, it can be inferred that the gun is possibly Croatian in origin.

The R9-Arms submachine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ramo RT-37
description:
Type: Gas Grenade/Flare Launcher

The Ramo RT-37 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: R-92
description:
The R-92 is a compact revolver produced by KBP Instrument Design Bureau of Tula. It was intended as a conceal carry, instant readiness revolver for Russia's internal security forces. As the ammunition used in the R-92 are all rimmless cartridges, the rounds are loaded into a removable metal clip, which is then removed and replaced for reloading. The first revolvers had a barrel in the spirit of Smith & Wesson Model 36 / 38, but since 1993 it has been replaced by a massive one with a wide ledge underneath.

The R-92 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: R wz. 42 "Sidolówka"
description:
The R wz. 42 "Sidolówka" was a fragmentation type high explosive hand grenade first produced in Warsaw 1942. The name Sidolówka came from Sidol, which was a metal cleaning agent produced by the company Henkel. The bottles of Sidol were used as casings for the grenades; this helped to disguise it. Around 350, 000 pieces were made, all of them in underground factories, and a lot of them were used by the Polish Resistance fighters during the Warsaw Uprising.

The R wz. 42 "Sidolówka" and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Raven Arms MP-25
description:
The Raven Arms MP-25 was among the first of many cheap, low-quality pistols produced by manufacturers in the Los Angeles and south-west Nevada areas in response to import restrictions of the 1968 GCA not applying to domestic manufacturer. After a factory fire at Raven Arms, recovered tooling would be purchased by a new company, Phoenix Arms, which would make a new version of the pistol, the Phoenix Arms Raven.

The Raven Arms MP-25 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rafael Mini-Spike
description:
The Rafael Mini-Spike is an offshoot of the full size Spike and is intended for use against personnel and light vehicles. Due to the small size and weight of the launcher and ammunition (4kg for the launcher and 4kg for each missile) it is intended that a large number of rounds can be carried by a small team, with the operator carrying the command module and two missiles and other members of the team each carrying an additional four missiles. The Mini-Spike utilises the same "Fire, Observe and Update" guidance system of the full size Spike. This means that the missile can either be used in a "fire-and-forget" mode, or can have the target updated, changed or aborted wirelessly from the control unit. This capability allows the Mini-Spike to be fired speculatively to look for a target which is acquired by the operator after launch. The warhead itself can also be deactivated after launch, turning the missile into a pure kinetic weapon when collateral damage is a concern.

The Rafael Mini-Spike and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RDG-1 smoke grenade
description:
The RDG-1 ( Ruchnaya dymovaya granata, smoke hand grenade) is a Soviet smoke grenade (during its production lifespan, it was simply known as the "RDG" and was retroactively designated the RDG-1 only after the RDG-2 was put into service). The grenade was designed in 1941 by K. Khess in besieged Leningrad. No less than 6. 87 million pieces were produced until the end of the war. In the 1950s, the RDG-1 was replaced by the more advanced RDG-2.

The RDG-1 smoke grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RDG-2 smoke grenade
description: The RDG-2 smoke grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RDG-M smoke grenade
description: The RDG-M & RGK-60KD and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Reck P6/P8
description:
A pair of nearly identical pocket pistols, the Reck P6 gas-firing pistol and Reck P8. 25 caliber pistol, were produced by Karl Arndt Reck Sportwaffenfabrik from the mid-50s to the late 60s. While the P6 was intended mostly for West Germany and other European states where gun laws allowed only less-lethal weapons, the P8 was extensively exported to the USA, where it was sold under the names LA Fury and Chicago Cub. This typical "Saturday Night Special" gun has a steel frame and Zamac zinc alloy slide. Several German companies also produced the Reck P8 under license. Among these versions are the Gecado Model 11 produced by G. C. Dornheim and imported to the USA by Hawes Firearms Company, and the Valor SM-11 produced by Indian Arms. These pistols, while internally the same, have differently placed safeties and the slide serration have different shapes. Less-lethal guns of the family also include starter the pistols P6S and P10, and an improved version of the gas pistol, the P800.

Internally, all these guns are based on the Walther Model 9 pistol.

name: Reichsrevolver M1879
description:
The Reichsrevolver M1879 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

Reichs-Commissions-Revolver Modell 1879, more often referred to as the Reichsrevolver M1879, was the service pistol of the Imperial German Army until 1908 when it was replaced by the Luger P08. Later, this revolver was used by non-frontline troops in WWI and even into WWII.

specs.Caliber: 10. 6x25R German Ordnance (also known as "10. 55mm German" (. 41 in)

name: Reibel Machine Gun
description:
The MAC 1931 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

The MAC 1934 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Reising Submachine Gun
description:
Adopted in early 1942 by the USMC, due largely to a shortage of the Thompson Submachine Gun, the Reising was never especially popular with the Marines since it proved to be a maintenance intensive weapon (something not always possible in the primitive Pacific theatre). It was so unpopular on Guadalcanal that Lt. Col. Merritt Edson, commanding officer of a Marine Raider battalion, ordered all Reising submachine guns issued to his unit to be dumped unceremoniously into the Lunga River, so that his men might draw better weapons such as the M1903 Springfield. (However, this was due to a maintenance error where the individually-fitted Reisings were disassembled en masse and acquired mismatched parts. The replacement Reisings were ordered to be maintained individually and problems were greatly reduced. Dumping the mismatched guns avoided persistent problems. )

One major design flaw in the weapon is the fact that the operating mechanism is a paddle in a slot on the underside of the forestock. If an unfortunate firer's finger slipped into this slot during full auto fire, breakage of bone could potentially result. This slot was also prone to accumulation of dirt or mud in the field, and even when clean made cocking the weapon awkward.

specs.Length: 35. 2 in (89. 5 cm) - M50 / 22. 2 in (56. 5 cm) - M55 with folded stock
specs.Capacity: 12 and 20-round box magazines, 30-round aftermarket magazines also exists

name: Remington 1863 Zouave
description:
The Remington 1863 Zouave is an American muzzleloader percussion rifle, that was invented during the American Civil War. About 20, 000 Remington Zouave rifles were produced, but was never used in any war or local conflict.

The Remington 1863 Zouave and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Remington 1875
description:
The Remington 1875 revolver is an improved version of the percussion Remington New Army, and was manufactured by Remington & Sons from 1875 to 1889. The Remington 1875 features a bored through cylinder to facilitate the usage of metallic cartridges, and like Remington's percussion revolvers, has an easily detachable cylinder. It was made as a competitor to the Colt Single Action Army and saw some success in that regard due to its sturdy quality.

The Remington 1875 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Remington Double Derringer
description:
The Remington derringer widely known as the Remington Double Derringer was manufactured 1866-1935 and is based on a patent held by William H. Elliot. Other popular names for this pistol are Elliot's Over/Under Derringer and (for derringers manufactured 1921-1935) Remington 95 Double Derringer.

Three types are distinguished by the manufacturer's stamping. There are several variants of each type based on the positions and style of the stamping.

name: Remington Mark III Flare Pistol
description:
The Remington Mark III Flare Pistol was manufactured between 1915 and 1918 with a total of 24, 500 pistol made. It is made out of brass and steel with mahogany grips. The Remington Mark III Flare Pistol is capable of firing 10ga flares and shot shells.

Barrel Length: 9 in (22. 86 cm)

name: Remington Model 11-48
description:
The Remington Model 11-48 is a recoil operated semi auto shotgun typically used in trapshooting or hunting first manufactured in 1949 and then discontinued in 1968. It was the first new shotgun to be introduced by Remington after World War II. Approximately 455, 535 were made between 1949 and 1968 in various Gauges.

The Remington Model 11-48 Shotgun appears in the following movies, TV shows, and video games:

specs.Caliber: 12 gauge, 16 gauge, 20 gauge, 28 gauge, and. 410 bore

name: Remington Model 10
description:
The Remington Model 10 is a pump-action shotgun with an internal hammer and tube magazine. The Model 10 was manufactured between 1908 and 1929, with a "trench gun" version used during World War I. Like the Ithaca 37, the Model 10 shotgun has no side ejection port, instead loading and ejecting from the bottom port. It was offered in 12 gauge with barrel lengths from 20 to 32 inches (51-81 cm).

Barrel Length: 20-32 in (51–81 cm)

name: Remington Model 11-87
description:
The Remington Model 11-87 is a gas-operating auto-loading shotgun. It has more than a dozen different variants and configurations.

The Remington Model 11-87 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Weight: 8. 25 lb (3. 7 kg) with 28 in (710 mm) barrel

name: Remington Model 11-96
description: The Remington Model 11-96 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Remington Model 1100
description:
The Remington Model 1100 is an American-manufactured semi-automatic shotgun with variants designed for hunting and law enforcement/military applications, first introduced in 1963 and still in production today.

The Remington Model 1100 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Remington Model 14
description:
The Remington Model 14 is a pump-action rifle designed by John Pedersen for the Remington Arms. Varians of the Model 14 include sporting Remington Model 14 1/2 and improved version Remington Model 141 Gamemaster. About 126, 000 Model 14 and Model 14 1/2 and about 77, 000 Model 141 were produced in total.

(1913 – 1934 (Model 14); 1914 – 1931 (Model 14-1/2); 1935 – 1950 (Model 141))

specs.Length: 42. 75 in (108. 6 cm) (Model 14 with 24 in (61 cm) barrel)

name: Remington Model 12A

name: Remington Model 30
description:
The Remington Model 30 is an American bolt-action sporting rifle manufactured by Remington. After the end of WWI and termination of contracts for both the P14 and 1917 Enfield rifles, Remington decided to use the tooling and the large inventory of spare parts stored in its two arsenal plants (Eddystone and Ilion) to make a sporting version of the Model 1917 Enfield rifles at the Ilion plant by refining the contour of the action, shortening the barrel, adding a sporter type stock and cock-on-open bolt action. In 1921, they introduced their Model 30 High Power in. 30-06 Springfield. It was Remington's first large-caliber, purpose-built sporting rifle. Remington built various grades and sight combination from 1921 until production stopped in 1940. Both Model 30 and the carbine variant stopped production in 1926 and were replaced by the upgraded Model 30 Express version. The Model 30 Express was available in. 30-06 Springfield, .25 Rem, .30 Rem, .32 Rem, .35 Rem, 7mm Mauser, and. 257 Roberts. The Model 30 was scheduled to be replaced by what would become the Remington Model 721 in 1940 but the war delayed the new model until 1948.

The Remington Model 30 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Caliber (s): .30-06 Springfield, .25 Rem, .30 Rem, .32 Rem, .35 Rem, 7mm Mauser, .257 Roberts.

name: Remington Model 31
description:
The Remington Model 31 is an American pump-action shotgun manufactured by Remington Arms. It is the successor to the bottom-ejecting John Browning designed Remington Model 17 pump-action shotgun, and is in essence a scaled-up Model 17 with a solid frame as opposed to a takedown design, along with side-ejection as opposed to bottom-ejection. It was conceived as a competitor to the hugely popular Winchester Model 1912. Model 31 "riot guns" were produced for the US military during World War II, but no true "trench guns" were mass produced. Although finely built and well-received, sales were still unable to compete with that of the Model 1912.

Remington ceased manufacturing the shotgun in 1949 due to its high manufacturing cost compared to its modest sales. Its cheaper to produce successor, the Remington Model 870, would go on be one of the most widely used shotguns in history.

description:
The Remington Model 7 is a compact tactical rifle with a scope and thumb cut stock. This rifle differs from the classic Remington 700 action in that the receiver is shorter than standard length and takes shorter, but still powerful, cartridges. It is designed to be a tactical bolt action rifle that is compact and can be easily fired from minimal cover. The Predator and CDL variants are "sportsman" versions and are promoted as a rifle for "thick cover hunting".

description:
Introduced in 1948, the Remington Model 721 was designed to replace the Remington Model 30 sporting rifle. The Model 721 was a Mauser based design and was a long action. Remington also manufactured the Model 722 in a short action for the. 308 Winchester, .222 Remington, .224 Remington, .257 Roberts and. 300 Savage cartridges. Both models were discontinued in 1962 due to the introduction of the Remington Model 700. 118, 000 units (Model 721 & Model 722) were manufactured between 1948-1962. Many are still in use today.

The Remington Model 721 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Remington Model 760/7600 Rifle
description:
The Remington Model 760 is a pump-action rifle that was first introduced in 1952. It was replaced in 1981 by the improved Model 7600, which was offered in 'sportsman' design with wooden furniture, and 'tactical' design with black synthetic forearm and buttstock. The "Police Model" of the 7600 shortened the Barrel from 22. 5" to 16. 5" and moved the rear sight from the barrel (above the foregrip) to the back of the receiver. In addition to the Model 7600 Police, Remington has also produced a 5. 56x45mm NATO/. 223 Remington version of the Model 7600P called the Model 7615P. Unlike other Model 7600 rifles, the Model 7615P has a magazine well designed to accept M16/AR-15 rifle magazines.

The Remington Model 760 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Remington Model 700
description:
The Remington Model 700 is the flagship sporting bolt action hunting rifle for Remington Arms. Introduced to the public in late 1962, it was a development of the previous sporting rifles, the Remington Model 721 and 722. The Model 721 was the first truly modern sporting rifle of the 20th century. The bolt action was the strongest of its kind when it was introduced in 1948 and was not just a sporterized version of a military surplus rifle. However, the 721 was only offered in long action.

It was available in. 264 Winchester, .270 Winchester, .280 Remington, .30-06, and. 300 H&H Mag. The 722 was developed for smaller game cartridges with a short action only - it was available in. 222 Remington, .222 Remington Magnum, .243 Winchester, .244 Remington, .257 Roberts, .300 Savage, and. 308 Winchester. Both the 721 and 722 rifles were discontinued in 1962 upon the introduction of the new and refined Model 700.

It was specifically designed for medium to moderately powered cartridges designed for pump or lever action rifles (like the. 30-30) and was never designed to take any of the higher powered cartridges.

name: Remington New Army
description:
The Remington New Army is a percussion revolver manufactured by Remington & Son and offered in. 36 (Navy) or. 44 (Army). It was used widely during the American Civil War from 1862 onwards. It was used primarily by Union soldiers, and widely favored over the standard issue Colt Army Model 1860 by those who could afford it, due primarily to its durability. The high numbers produced for the war and (officially) being a non-standard pattern made it common on the surplus market, with Bannerman still offering them for cheap ($1. 75 in 1903, less than a new single shot. 22 rifle) into the early 1900s. This ensured it saw use in the American West and beyond, both in its original percussion configuration and as a metallic cartridge conversion. While reproductions can quickly swap out cylinders for rapid reloads, the hand-fitted nature of the originals' cylinders makes this dubiously plausible at best.

Note that the New Army is often referred to as the "Remington 1858 New Army", or sometimes the "Remington Model 1858" (with similar names applied to its various derivatives, like the New Police below); while Remington never used "1858" as part of the gun's designation, it is present as part of a patent date marking, which has led to the popular misconception that it was a model designation. This has become so pervasive that many modern reproductions are actually designated "1858", only furthering the confusion.

specs.Cartridge: Powder & ball or paper cartridge with conical bullet

name: Remington R4
description:
The Remington R4 is Remington's version of the M4 / M16 rifle series, produced in part out of spite after legal action by Colt cost Remington a contract to produce 24, 000 M4A1 Carbines for the US Army in 2012. Following the failure of this contract (due to the US Army awarding the full order for 120, 000 rifles to FN Herstal rather than Colt or Remington), Remington were awarded a major contract to supply 50, 000 R4 rifles to the Philippine Army, as a replacement for their stocks of Cold War-vintage M16s.

There are four main variants: the compact "C" with a 7-inch barrel, the 11. 5-inch "E" entry carbine, the standard 14. 5-inch carbine, and a 20-inch rifle version. A gas piston rifle developed from the R4 (which uses direct impingement just like the M16 / M4 series), the Remington R5 RGP, was also unveiled in 2012.

name: Remington Model 870
description:
The Remington Model 870 shotgun debuted in 1950 and has been used by police and military ever since as well as hunters, sportsmen and gun enthusiasts. The Remington Model 870 is arguably the world's most widely owned and used shotgun, with over 9 million in civilian hands.

For information on how to tell the Remington Model 870 series shotguns apart from Mossberg and Winchester shotguns, please visit the 12 Gauge Pump Shotgun page.

description:
The RSASS (Remington Semi Automatic Sniper System) is a modern 7. 62x51mm sniper system, developed by Remington Arms in collaboration with JP Enterprises. In January 2012, the rifle was renamed to R11 RSASS, and became part of Remington's "R-series" of rifles.

The Remington RSASS can be seen in the following:

name: Remington R51
description:
The Remington R51 is a modern version of the John Pedersen Remington Model 51 pistol. The new R51 is made without sharp corners, flat and lightweight, maximally suited for concealed carry. The pistol grip with a large angle of inclination has excellent ergonomics, which provides high accuracy and accuracy of "instinctive" offhand shooting. Trigger type trigger mechanism, single action, with hidden location of the trigger. In addition, there is no safety catch on the Model 51.

The layout of the Remington R51 is similar to the Walther PP pistol, as it has a barrel rigidly fixed in a frame, around which a return spring is placed. A feature of this pistol is the use of John Douglas Pederson locking - a semi-free bolt with a barrel bore locking by friction between the bolt and the casing. This system was originally designed with the. 45 ACP cartridge in mind. R51 shutter and casing are separate parts.

name: Remington Versa Max

name: Remington Rolling Block
description:
The Remington Rolling Block is a single-shot, breech-loading rifle that was produced by Remington. First designed by Joseph Rider in 1864, the original Rolling Block design was finalized too late to see action in the American Civil War. However, it would go on to see extensive military use throughout the later half of the 1800s. Militarized Rolling Blocks would be produced in numerous calibers and would see use by Sweden, Norway, Denmark, Spain, Egypt, Argentina, and many other nations. The Rolling Block was also very popular on the civilian market and was prized by hunters. Its popularity owed in part to its very strong and robust action, which could handle the increased pressure generated by the new smokeless powder cartridges that came into common use in the late 1800s. Rolling Blocks were even used in a military capacity up until World War I, when Britain bought surplus stock to arm personnel of Royal Navy support vessels and France issued Rolling Blocks to rear line troops.

The name refers to the action the rifle uses- rolling block action. In this type of firearm action, the breech is sealed by a circular breechblock that rotates or rolls on a pin.

name: Remington-Keene Repeating Rifle
description:
Remington manufactured the rifle from 1880 - 1885 with only around 5, 000 rifles produced. One of the least known, but quite interesting, rifles of this type is the Remington-Keene U. S. Navy rifle. The rifle was the invention of New Jersey arms designer John W. Keene. Keene had been working on his bolt-action repeating rifle since the early 1870s and eventually received nine separate patents pertaining to the design. The rifle's turning bolt operated in more or less the typical fashion. The action was unlocked by raising the bolt upward, and the empty case was extracted and ejected by pulling the bolt to the rear. As the bolt was almost fully retracted, the "magazine elevator" (as it was called by the inventor) raised a fresh cartridge from the tubular magazine into position in the breech so it could be chambered when the bolt was pushed forward. Manipulation of the bolt did not automatically cock the cocking piece (which was fashioned in the shape of an external hammer); instead, lowered it to the "half-cock" position (which functioned as a safety), and the user would then have to manually re-cock the rifle. The "hammer" was one of the more recognizable components of the design and was viewed as a safety feature, as soldiers unfamiliar with repeating bolt-action rifles of the era sometimes had trouble ascertaining if a rifle was cocked. With the Keene rifle's external hammer-shaped cocking piece, one could easily observe its position to determine if the rifle was fully cocked and ready to fire.

The rifle's tubular magazine was under the barrel and was encased in-and protected by-the stock's fore-end. The location of the magazine required that the cleaning rod be offset to the left side of the fore-end. As with most of the other bolt-action military repeating rifles of the time, the Keene was fitted with a magazine cut-off to permit it to function as a single-shot, with the contents of the magazine held in reserve for "emergency" use.

name: Rexim Favor
description: The Rexim Favor submachine gun was designed in Switzerland in 1950s by Rexim S. A. and produced in Spain by La Coruna in 1955-1957. About 5, 000 SMGs were produced. The only state that used the Rexim Favor was Turkey.

name: RG-14 hand grenade
description:
The RG-14 (Russian: РГ-14, Ручная граната Рдултовского образца 1914 года, R uchnaja g ranata Rdultovskogo obrazca 19 14 goda, "Hand Grenade Pattern of year 1914"), known in English sources as Model 1914 grenade, is a Russian hand grenade developed by Capt. Vladimir Iosifovich Rdultovsky, based his previous RG-12 hand grenade. It was the main hand grenade of the Russian Imperial Army during the Great War, and of the Red Army during the Russian Civil war.

In 1925, the decision was made to create a new stick hand grenade. The first trials of what later became RGD-33 were unsuccessful, and modified RG-14s were adopted as a stopgap weapon. The modified version, designated RG-14/33, had increased capacity and reliability. It was filled with TNT instead of Picric Acid and to improve its kill radius, a fragmentation sleeve could be fitted (the sleeve was optional, placed on the grenade only when needed during combat). Otherwise, the RG-14/30 is exactly the same as the base model. The RG-14/33 was produced in 1930-1934 and then replaced in production by the RGD-33, but large stores of these grenades, both newly produced and converted from original RG-14s, allowed its usage in the Red Army until mid-1943.

specs.Weight: 1. 5 lbs (0. 7 kg); (1. 8 lbs (0. 8 kg) with a fragmentation sleeve)
specs.Blast radius: 5 - 20 meters (16 - 66 ft), 25-100 meters (90- 109 yds) with a fragmentation sleeve

name: RG-12 hand grenade
description: The RG-12 (Russian: РГ-12, Ручная граната Рдултовского образца 1912 года, "Hand Grenade Pattern of year 1912"), also known in English sources as the M1912 Lantern grenade, is an early Russian hand grenade.

name: RGD-33 stick grenade
description:
RGD-33 Grenade was an anti-personnel fragmentation stick hand grenade used during World War II as the main grenade used by the Red Army. It was complicated to use and manufacture. Following the German invasion of the USSR, the crude and simple RG-42 was designed to slowly replace it.

The RGD-33 stick grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RG-42 hand grenade
description:
The RG-42 was a fragmentation grenade designed in the Soviet Union during WWII, when production was not sufficient to supply the original pre-war hand grenades. It was essentially a redesign of the short-lived RG-41 grenade to use the UZRGM universal grenade fuze, also used on the F-1 hand grenade and later the RGD-5. The production is a very simple design compared to the complicated RGD-33 stick grenade. After the war, the grenade continued to be produced in Poland and China. It was eventually replaced in Soviet use by the RGD-5, starting in 1954.

The RG-42 hand grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Weight: 384 g (420 g with fuze), filler 200g TNT

name: Republic Arms Musler
description:
The Musler is a series of copies of the Beretta Model RS-202P shotguns, produced in South Africa by Musgrave. The name is a portmanteau of the names of the two enterprises Musgrave and Hausler (the latter was engaged in development). Various sources may refer to this as Musgrave, Musler, and Hausler, although these are technically the same weapon. The Musler shotgun was created (in 1987 - 1989) to complement the Beretta RS-202P, which South African police have used since 1985. The police wanted the replacement weapon to keep the RS202P's overall original design. At the same time, the customer requested changes to the design of the new weapon, the most urgent of which was the possibility of throwing rifle gas and smoke grenades without the use of additional devices on the shotgun. In the design, this is expressed by the fact that the front sight on the barrel and the fastening of the magazine tube are moved back, allowing you to "put on" a grenade on the barrel.

While the original shotguns had wooden furniture, the later ones have the modern black synthetic one. They can also be fitted with folding stocks, like the Beretta RS-202 M1/M2. There are also versions for commercial sales, without the aforementioned changes, structurally similar to the original Beretta.

name: RG-6 grenade launcher
description:
The RG-6 (also known by its GRAU index 6G30 ) is a Russian multi-shot grenade launcher. It fires 40mm caseless grenades from a six-shot cylinder. It was developed in 1993 by TsKIB SOO, a subsidiary of KBP Instrument Design Bureau.

The RG-6 grenade launcher and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RGS-50M
description:
The RGS 50M is a Russian grenade launcher manufactured by V. A. Degtyarev Plant. It is an improvement of the earlier Soviet RGS-50; featuring a foregrip, an improved trigger and improved stock. It was primarily designed to fire less lethal ammunition; but can also fire explosive grenades.

The RGS-50M and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RGO/RGN fragmentation grenade
description:
The RGO and RGN (also known as 7G22 and 7G21 by GRAU index) are Soviet/Russian hand grenades, respectively, anti-personnel defensive and offensive. They were developed in the GNPP "Bazalt" design bureau, by S. Korshunov and V. Kuzmin in the late 1970s. In 1981, the RGO and RGN were introduced into service. However, they were never able to replace the RGD-5 and F-1 due to an overabundance of already available grenades, and RGO/RGN are mainly used by special forces.

The RGO/RGN fragmentation grenade appears in the following films and video games used by the following actors:

name: Ribeyrolles 1918 Automatic Carbine
description:
The Ribeyrolles 1918 Automatic Carbine is an Automatic Carbine/Light Machine Gun produced in France in 1916. The design looks similar to an SMG, but at the time was used as a LMG and Automatic Rifle. The weapon was produced from 1918 to 1920. It utilized a 25 round magazine and fired between 550 and 600 rounds per minute. The weapon was capable of equipping a bi-pod and a bayonet. Its ammunition, the 8x35mm Ribeyrolles, is a unique intermediate cartridge obtained by mating the projectile of the 8mm Lebel round to a necked down casing of the. 351 Winchester Self-Loading round, and was argued by some to be the first purpose-built intermediate cartridge, making the carbine one of the first assault rifles by some definitions.

Despite having features that are ahead of its time, it never saw any service in the war due to having a heavy design and a weak round that were unsuitable for its role as an LMG, and more importantly because it was incredibly unreliable and prone to jamming.

name: Rifle Dynamics RD700 rifle series
description: The RD 700 is a series of AK rifles by Rifle Dynamics. The series includes Rifle, Pistol, Hunter and Ban State models. All guns are equipped with pistol grips from Magpul or Tango Down.

specs.Type: Rifle, Carbine (the stockless carbine versions are legally pistols)

name: Rheinmetall Rh 202
description:
The Rheinmetall Rh 202 is a 20mm autocannon manufactured by Rheinmetall AG since the 1960s. It was adopted by the Bundeswehr as both an AA cannon and as a weapon for ground vehicles; in the latter role, it serves as the main weapon of the Marder IFV and Wiesel 1A4 reconnaissance vehicle, along with the Argentinian VCTP IFV. Other notable uses include its role as a secondary weapon on the prototype MBT-70/KPz 70 tanks, and as a naval gun on smaller ships like the LÉ Orla used by New Zealand.

The Rheinmetall Rh 202 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RM-38 Mortar
description:
RM-38 (known also under the names M-38 and 50-RM-38 ) is a Soviet 50mm mortar. The index "RM" means "rotny minomyot" - the mortar for company service. The mortar was designed in 1936-1938 and put into service in Summer 1938. Slightly modified RM-39 and RM-40 versions appeared in two following years. More than 24, 000 mortars of all three versions were produced. In 1941 they were replaced by RM-41, inspired by German Granatwerfer 36 Mortar. In 1943 50-mm mortars of all versions were removed from service of Red Army due to the low efficiency.

name: RMB-93
description:
The RMB-93 (also known as "Rys", meaning "Lynx") is a Russian pump-action shotgun conceived for close-quarter-combat with an action similar to the NeoStead 2000 Shotgun, as it is pumped forward-back rather than the usual back-forward, and the magazine located above the barrel. "RMB" is stands for Ruzhyo Magazinnoe Boevoe (Magazine-fed Combat Shotgun). The RMB-93 was one of the Russian combat shotguns designed for police but never widely used (although it found use in the Belarusian KGB Spetsnaz), however, it gained some popularity with the advent of civilian models in 1996.

Civilian RMB-93 models are known as RMO-93, Ruzhyo Magazinnoe Ohotnichye (Magazine-fed Hunting Shotgun). The RMO-93s were suggested for use in different configurations, with a variety of barrels and stocks, although magazine capacity is not adjustable. There was also an experimental underbarrel shortened version (in the spirit of the Knight's Armament Masterkey ) for use with the 9A-91. In 2007, manufacture of all RMB-93 variants ceased, except for bespoke models still produced in small quantities.

specs.Length: From 25. 9 in (65. 7 cm) with folded stock to 42. 5 in (108 cm) with unfolded/fixed stock, on various versions

name: RMN-50
description:
The RMN-50 (Russian: РМН-50; Р учной м иномёт Н аймана, калибр 50 мм; Ruchnoy minomyot Naymana, kalibr 50 mm; "Naiman Hand Mortar, caliber 50mm") is a Soviet hand mortar/grenade launcher developed during the World War II by a team of engineers under the lead of Isaac Naiman for partisan operations.

The RMN-50 is based on the Mosin Nagant rifle and the RM-38/RM-40 mortar; damaged Mosin Nagant rifles were modified with barrels from the mortars to create the RMN-50. The rifle's iron sights are relocated to the new mortar barrel. The RMN-50 fired 50mm mortar shells with their the propelling charges removed, and used 7. 62x54mmR blanks to launch the shells.

name: Robar SR-21
description: The Robar SR-21 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Robar Melior
description:
Melior is a trade name for series of pistols, produced by Belgian company Robar of Liège. These pistols were manufactured in two versions, First Model and New Model, both in 7. 65mm and 6. 35mm calibers. Robar pistols are also known under trade names Jieffeco (pre-WW1 sales) and Mercury (this name was used for. 22 caliber version of New Model for the sale in USA after 1945).

(First Model: 1910-1920; New Model: 1920-? ?? ?)

description: The Rock Island Armory VR-80 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rocky Mountain Arms Patriot
description: The Rocky Mountain Arms Patriot and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:
