description:
Total length: 18. 2 in (46. 3 cm)

The Rock River Arms LAR-PDS can be seen in the following:

name: Rocky Mountain Arms Stakeout
description: The Rocky Mountain Arms Stakeout and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rohr Handgranate
description:
The Rohr Handgranate is an Austro-Hungarian fragmentation grenade developed before WWI; it is a fragmentation grenade made up of a cast iron body filled with steel balls and a cardboard handle, the weapon was designed to be thrown from cover. In the Spring of 1915, a new version based on the German Model 15 Stielhandgranate was introduced; the new model was a concussive grenade, the "ball" body was replaced with a cast iron cylinder filled with explosives.

The Rohr Handgranate and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Roger & Spencer
description:
The Roger & Spencer Revolver was produced in Willowvale (NY) between 1863-1865. It is a. 44 caliber cap and ball revolver, with a 7 ½" octagon barrel. 5, 800 revolvers were manufactured, but too late to be used in the Civil War.

Current replicas are very appreciated, because of their great quality and accuracy.

name: ROKS flamethrowers
description:
ROKS ( Rantseviy Ognemyot Klyueva-Sergeeva, literally "Knapsack Flamethrower by Klyuev and Sergeev") is a series of Soviet man-portable flamethrowers. The first model, ROKS-1, was designed in early 1930s but wasn't put into service. The second version ROKS-2 was issued in mid-1930s and used by Soviet Army in Winter War and WW2. In 1942 it was replaced with the improved ROKS-3.

The fuel and gas tanks (capacity: 9 litres (2. 4 US gal)) of ROKS flamethrowers were concealed in a metal casting, resembling an ordinary soldier knapsack, and the flame projector resembled a standard Mosin Nagant Rifle, so the operator of the flamethrower generally looked the same as other soldiers until the weapon fires. The fuel was propelled by pressurized nitrogen gas and ignited by firing specially modified 7. 62x25mm TT cartridges: this was a necessity as gas torch-ignited flamethrowers perform poorly in extremely cold conditions. The effective firing range was 25 m (27. 3 yards), and maximum around 45 m (50 yards).

name: Rodman Model 1861
description:
The Rodman Model 1861, often known simply as the Rodman gun, was the designation for a trio of large smoothbore cannons from the American Civil War era, intended for use in coastal fortifications. The Model 1861 was produced in 8-inch, 10-inch, and 15-inch varieties, nearly identical aside from their increasing size. In contrast to the over eighteen-hundred Model 1861s produced, there was also a grand total of three Rodman Model 1864 guns made, one 13-inch and two 20-inch. Nearly two-hundred Rodman guns survive today, including both 20-inch guns.

The Rodman Model 1861 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rohrbaugh R9
description:
The Rohrbaugh R9 is a semi-automatic pistol that was produced by Rohrbaugh Firearms of Long Island, New York. It's chambered in either 9x19mm Parabellum or. 380 ACP, and was designed to be a lightweight, compact self-defense weapon.

The Rohrbaugh R9 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rossi Circuit Judge
description:
The Rossi Circuit Judge is a family of revolving, swing-out cylinder rifles, capable of firing both. 410 bore shells and. 45 LC ammo. It is available with hard wood or synthetic furniture. In addition to the adjustable rear and fiber optic front sights, it may be equipped with a Picatinny rail that allows for attachment of a choice of optics. In 2011, a variant for. 22 Long Rifle and. 22 Magnum was introduced. This version is based on the Taurus Tracker 992 revolver action and comes with both a. 22 LR cylinder and a. 22 Magnum cylinder. This model features a thumbhole synthetic stock with a forward under-barrel Picatinny rail.

The Rossi Circuit Judge and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ross Rifle
description:
The Canadian Ross Rifle shares a dubious distinction alongside the Chauchat of being one of the worst weapons deployed during World War I. The straight-pull bolt-action Ross was initially designed by Scottish-born Sir Charles Ross, inspired by the Mannlicher M1895, as a target rifle for the civilian market. It was initially pitched to the Royal Canadian Mounted Police in its Mk I form, with 1, 000 rifles being sent for testing in 1905: this led to rejection in 1906, with the RCMP producing a list of 113 defects (it is, needless to say, unusual for a rifle to have more defects than components), including a defective bolt lock that allowed the entire bolt to simply fall out of the rifle and internal springs so badly tempered they were described as being "soft as copper. " This led to rejection and the redesigned Mk II in 1905, later reinforced as the Mk II* to handle the new. 280 round.

In 1910, another new version was produced, the Mk III, also known as the M1910 or Model 10. While it was hopeless as a standard infantry arm, it found a place with snipers, who tended to be able to take better care of their weapon and appreciated the greater accuracy over the SMLE, though they were less keen on its tendency to jam if fed ammunition that was not perfectly clean. While Canadian Minister of Militia and Defence Sam Hughes attempted to turn the matter into an issue of national pride and repeatedly ignored reports of serious issues with the Ross, eventually the evidence against the gun proved overwhelming: Hughes was forced to resign in 1916, and most Ross Rifles were pulled back from the front line to be used as training weapons in order to free up Lee-Enfields for use in combat. The configuration called Mk III B was a British variant with minimal differences, mostly confined to the sights. 100, 000 were ordered, with the order being cancelled after 67, 000, these also ending up being used as training rifles. On their entry into the war, the US military received stocks of Ross Rifles for the same purpose.

specs.Capacity: 5 round integral box magazine loaded with charger clips
specs.Variants: Mark I (1903), Mark II (1905), Mark II*. 280 (1907), Mark III (1910), Mark IIIB (1914)

name: Rossi Model 51
description: The Rossi Model 51 can be seen in the following productions:

name: Rossi Model 68
description: The Rossi Model 68 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rossi Model 851
description:
The Rossi Model 851 (also known as the M851) is an economical Brazilian revolver based on the Colt Diamondback. It comes in a blued and stainless version.

The Rossi Model 851 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Rossi Model 988
description:
The Rossi Model 988 "Cyclops" is offered in caliber. 357 Magnum only. The revolver is double action and it fires six rounds per load. It is available in stainless steel only. There are four compensator ports on each side of the barrel near the muzzle.

The Rossi Model 988 revolver can be seen in the following films used by the following actors:

name: Rossi Overland
description:
The Overland was manufactured between 1978 to 1994 by Rossi and imported by Amadeo. Imported By Braztech. It was manufactured in a variety of gauges including. 410 Bore (Approximately 67-68GA), 12GA, 20GA. The Rossi Overland was a very popular shotgun among Cowboy Action Shooters. Its' economical price and classical exposed hammer styling made it popular in using as a vintage shotgun.

The Rossi Overland appears in the following movies, TV shows, and video games:

name: Rossi Model 971
description:
The Rossi Model 971 is a blue-steel single/double-action revolver offered in. 357 Magnum caliber only.

Along with the 971 Rossi also produced the Model 972. This is a polished stainless-steel version of the 971. Both revolvers have a rubberized finger grove grip and adjustable rear sight (both windage and elevation) with a sight radius of five fifteen-sixteenths inches. The 971 and 972 were manufactured in Brazil by Taurus Firearms Ltd. under license from Rossi USA.

name: Rossi R352
description: The Rossi R352 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Roth-Steyr M1907
description:
The Roth-Steyr M1907 was a Czech-designed semi-automatic pistol that was issued to the Austro-Hungarian Kaiserliche und Königliche Armee cavalry prior to and during World War I. With its introduction in 1907, it was one of the first pistols ever to become an official service weapon of an armed force. The Roth-Steyr replaced the Rast & Gasser revolvers and was intended primarily for cavalry. For this role, the Austro-Hungarian military required specific design features. It had to be ensured that a mounted soldier could not accidentally discharge the weapon he carried, even if his horse shied. This included the fact that the M1907 had no hammer. Instead, when the pistol was charged, the firing-pin spring was partially tensioned and the firing pin initially locked into place. Full tensioning occurred only when the soldier pulled the characteristically heavy military trigger. However, this was not a double-action system; in the event of a misfire, the weapon had to be re-cocked using the knob. This trigger system was later revisited in modern hammerless polymer pistols such as the Glock.

Typical of Steyr pistols of that era was the magazine permanently integrated into the grip frame, which had to be loaded using stripper clips. The knob at the rear of the pistol was used for loading. The very long breechblock bears little resemblance to a conventional slide. At the rear, it consists of a solid block (except for the firing-pin channel), and at the front, of a sleeve that surrounds the barrel. Spiral-shaped grooves were machined into the breech assembly, causing the barrel to rotate 90° under recoil, thereby unlocking it. The design was not fully matured. It occasionally happened that a second round was fired immediately after the first. Although this flaw was corrected, manufacturing by G. Roth AG proved too complex and was discontinued. Steyr then developed the successor model, the Steyr M1912, with an external hammer. To differentiate the two, that pistol is also called the "Steyr-Hahn", whereas this one is known as the "Steyr-Roth". A total of 80, 000 to 90, 000 units were produced, two-thirds in Steyr and the remainder at Fegyvergyár ("Arms Factory") in Budapest.

name: RPG-18
description: The RPG-18 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Length: 27. 8 in (70. 5 cm) (unarmed), 41. 3 in (105 cm) (ready to fire)

name: Rossi Princess
description:
The Rossi Princess is a Brazilian revolver based on the Smith & Wesson. 32 Ladysmith. The revolver's frame is made of Zamak (a cheap zinc alloy) while the barrel and cylinder are made of steel. In 1965, small changes were made to the design and models intended for export with shorter barrels and oxidized finishes were offered. Some Rossi Princesses were exported to the United States in the late 1960s until the Gun Control Act of 1968 banned their importation. Following this, the remaining export guns were sold in Brazil.

The Rossi Princess and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RPG-2
description:
The RPG-2 (Russian: РПГ-2, Ручной противотанковый гранатомёт, Ruchnoy Protivotankovy Granatomyot; English: "manually operated antitank grenade launcher") is a Soviet shoulder-fired rocket launcher, the forerunner to the ubiquitous RPG-7. It is the successor to the earlier, unsuccessful RPG-1. Introduced in 1949, it, like the RPG-1, is based on Soviet studies of the German Panzerfaust and American Bazooka rocket launchers. While light and compact enough to be comfortably used by a single person, in practice, at least two people were needed to operate the weapon at maximum effectiveness. The RPG-2 did not make it to the Korean War because the first version had some shortcomings that had to be corrected before actual combat use, leading to the widespread belief in the West that the RPG-2 did not exist before 1954.

Its low cost and simplicity lead to the RPG-2 being widely produced by the Soviet Union and exported, with other nations producing licensed copies of the launcher. Copies have been produced in China under the designation Type 56, (not to be confused with the Norinco Type 56 Carbine, Norinco Type 56 assault rifle, or the Norinco Type 56 light machine gun ), Vietnam as the B40 and B50, Somalia, Yugoslavia as the M57, and Czechoslovakia as the P-27. The RPG-2 was extensively used against US forces during the Vietnam War and saw use throughout Cold War conflicts like the Nigerian Civil War, Rhodesian Bush War, Portuguese Colonial War, Salvadoran Civil War, Lebanese Civil War, and Somali Civil War. The launcher was produced until 1960, when concerns over short effective range and poor accuracy led to the introduction of the RPG-7 in 1961.

specs.Weight: 6. 2 lbs (2. 8 kg) (unloaded), 10. 3 lbs (4. 7 kg) (ready to fire)

name: RPG-22
description: The RPG-22 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Length: 30. 9 in (78. 5 cm) (unarmed), 33. 5 in (85 cm) (ready to fire)

name: RPG-26
description:
The RPG-26 Aglen is a disposable anti-tank rocket launcher developed by the Soviet Union. It fires a single-stage rocket with jack-knife fins, which unfold after launch. The rocket carries a 72. 5 millimeter diameter high explosive anti-tank single shaped charge warhead capable of penetrating 440 millimeters of armour, one meter of reinforced concrete or one and a half meters of brickwork. It has a maximum effective range of around 250 meters.

The RPG-26 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RPG-29
description:
The RPG-29 "Vampir" is a Soviet unguided reusable rocket-propelled grenade launcher. The RPG-29 is breech-loaded instead of muzzle-loaded.

Caliber: 105 mm; 65/105mm warheads

name: RPG-27
description:
The RPG-27 "Tavolga" ("meadow grass") is a Soviet-designed single-shot disposable rocket launcher. Designed in the late 1980s and put into production and service in 1989, it was meant to help deal with advances in tank armor; it is similar in design to the previous RPG-26, but considerably larger, with a 105mm tandem-charge HEAT warhead capable of piercing upwards of 600mm of RHA.

In the late 1990s, a variant with a thermobaric warhead would be developed, under the name RShG-1.

name: RPG-28
description:
The RPG-28 Klyukva (Russian: Клюква, lit. "cranberry") is a Russian disposable rocket launcher unveiled at IDEX Abu Dhabi in 2007 and adopted by the Russian Army in 2011. The RPG-28 fires a 125mm tandem shaped charge, able to penetrate over 900mm of armor behind ERA, 3000mm of brickwork, or 2500mm of reinforced concrete, and has an effective range of 300m.

The RPG-28 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RPG-32
description:
The RPG-32 Hashim is a Russian reloadable anti-tank rocket launcher originally designed for the Jordanian Army. It consists of a short tube which incorporates the sighting unit, fire controls and grips to which a disposable tube containing either a 105mm or 72mm rocket is attached.

The RPG-32 Hashim and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Weight: 22 lbs (10 kg) with 105mm rocket, 13. 2 lbs (6 kg) with 72mm rocket
specs.Length: 47. 2 in (120 cm) with 105mm rocket, 35. 4 in (90 cm) with 72mm rocket

name: RPG-NH-75
description:
The RPG-NH-75 is a Czechoslovakian made single shot disposable anti-armour weapon. It is inspired by the M72 LAW, but unlike the Soviet produced copies such as the RPG-18 and RPG-22, it can be distinguished by its lack of an exterior "rail" for extending the tube, and by the design of the folding sights which are offset to the side rather than being in-line down the centre of the tube.

The RPG-NH-75 has appeared in the following:

name: RPG-43 Anti-tank Grenade
description:
The RPG-43 anti-tank grenade was a high explosive anti-tank hand grenade used by the USSR during the WWII. The RPG-43 used a shaped charge HEAT warhead.

The RPG-43 Anti-tank Grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RPG-40 anti-tank grenade
description:
RPG-40 is an anti-tank hand grenade used by the USSR during WWII. It was designed in 1940 and produced from 1941-1943. The grenade was also used in the Korean War. Unlike most anti-tank grenades that rely on shaped charges to penetrate armor, the RPG-40 is simply an oversize hand grenade relying on the blast of its massive explosive charge (0. 76kg/1. 7lb of TNT) to damage armor.

In the movies a training version of RPG-40 can be seen. It is colored in dull black, while live grenades are colored in green or khaki.

name: RPG-7
description:
The RPG-7 (Russian: РПГ-7, Ручной противотанковый гранатомёт, Ruchnoy Protivotankovy Granatomyot; English: "manually operated antitank grenade launcher", with "manually-operated" generally being translated as "handheld") has been utilized by both Soviet/Russian soldiers and all other Communist bloc countries and client states/groups throughout the world. Developed from the RPG-2 (also known as the B40 rocket launcher in Vietnam), the RPG-7 is seen all over the world - from the war zones of Africa and the Middle East to Central and South America.

The Chinese made their own version of the rocket - the Type 69 RPG, which differed in that there was only one pistol grip. The second pistol grip of the RPG-7 was replaced by a upper handle. Also the Type 69 has an integral lightweight bipod that folds against the body of the tube. In movies, many times the Type 69 RPG is seen rather than the Russian RPG-7.

name: RPO-A 'Shmel'
description:
The RPO-A "Shmel" ("Bumblebee") is a Russian man-portable rocket launcher (although it is classified as a flamethrower by the manufacturer) which first entered service in 1988. The RPO-A is a follow-on to the RPO "Rys" ("Lynx") napalm rocket launcher first deployed in 1975. The former weapon was based on Soviet observations of the American M202 FLASH. Like the FLASH, the RPO Rys was designed to replace obsolete and dangerous infantry flamethrowers (with the last such weapon in the Soviet arsenal being the LPO-50 flamethrower ) with a longer-ranged weapon that had the same capabilities against dug-in positions and structures. It was copied by China as the PF-97.

Unlike the RPO Rys, which was a crew-served reloadable launcher loaded with encased missiles from the rear like the French LRAC F1, the RPO-A is a single-shot, self-contained disposable launcher operated by a single crewman. They are either issued singly with a shoulder strap or in a two-launcher "transit pack" worn as a backpack. All models are externally similar, with three rocket variants. The basic rocket is the RPO-A, which has a thermobaric warhead, the RPO-Z is an incendiary warhead with a "pyrogel" filler that burns at around 1, 500-1, 800 degrees Fahrenheit (800-1, 000 degrees Celsius), and the RPO-D has a slightly heavier smoke-producing warhead. Since the RPO-D is not advertised as being incendiary, this is probably either hexachloroethane-zinc (HC) or terephthalic acid (TPA) rather than phosphorous.

specs.Weight: System: 26. 5 lbs (12 kg) Warhead weights: RPO-A: 4. 6 lbs (2. 1 kg) thermobaric, RPO-Z: 4. 6 lbs (2. 1 kg) incendiary, RPO-D: 5. 1 lbs (2. 3 kg) smoke
specs.Range: Minimum: 66 ft (20m), Effective: 660 ft (200m), Maximum: 3, 280 ft (1, 000m)

name: RPK
description:
NOTE: Some films use the Valmet M78 to impersonate the RPK. please see the Valmet M78 page for differences between these two weapons. The Zastava M72 is also on the M70 series page.

The RPK and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RSA Revolver
description:
The OTs-01 Kobalt (Russian: ОЦ-01 "Кобальт", "Cobalt") and also known as RSA (Revolver Stechkina-Avraamova) is a Russian revolver designed by Igor Stechkin, famous inventor of the APS automatic pistol. The revolver is also manufactured by "Zlatoust Machine-Building Plant" and "Uralsk Machine-Building Plant" under designation of TKB-0216.

Manufacturer: KBP Instrument Design Bureau

name: Ruger 22 Charger
description:
The Ruger 22 Charger is an American pistol manufactured by Ruger. It is the popular Ruger 10/22 configured to be a "pistol" under US firearms laws.

The Ruger 22 Charger and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: RT-20
description: The RT-20 is a single-shot Croatian bullpup anti-materiel rifle developed by RH-Alan during the Yugoslavian War. The name derives from "Ručni Top", which means "Handheld Cannon", with the 20 indicating the caliber.

name: RSh-12
description:
The RSh-12 (Russian: РШ-12; Револьвер Штурмовой калибра 12, 7, "12. 7mm Assault Revolver") is a somewhat mysterious Russian large-caliber revolver. It was stated that it was developed in the early 2000s. To public knowledge, its first and only appearance ever was a showcase in a 2014 convention, shown by the KBP Instrument Design Bureau. The model shown is an apparently stress-tested prototype (as indicated by numerous dents across the gun), marked with serial number 100001. This model is the only publicly-known model of the weapon. Only one image was taken of the weapon, accompanied with its information card (from which most technical info on the gun is taken from). In 2016, it was mentioned about its adoption by some special subdivisions of Russia, but so far there is no evidence of its actual use, as well as production.

The RSh-12 is often described as an anti-materiel revolver, and its was speculated to have been made for destroying locks and other objects. It is chambered in 12. 7x55mm, which is also used on the VSSK Vykhlop sniper rifle and the ShAK-12 battle rifle (previously known as the ASh-12. 7). While not a. 50 BMG, on a handgun scale, such an overkill round would be either for big game hunting or anti-materiel use; its tactical features suggest the latter.

name: Ruger American Rifle
description:
The Ruger American Rifle is a budget-level hunting/sporting bolt action centerfire and rimfire rifle made by Sturm, Ruger & Co. The stock is a polymer composite and the model is available in both stainless and blued steel.

The Ruger American Rifle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Capacity: 10-round rotary box magazine, 15-round Ruger BX-15 box magazine, 25-round Ruger BX-25 box magazine, 50-round drum magazine (. 22 LR); 9-round JMX-1 rotary box magazine, 15-round Ruger MX-15 Magnum box magazine (. 17 HMR &. 22 WMR)

name: Ruger 10/22
description:
The Ruger 10/22 is a very popular sporting weapon, named based on its. 22 caliber and standard 10-round magazine capacity (however it can take a variety of high capacity magazines). The gun is very popular in customization and many aftermarket parts can be bought to customize the rifle. Heavily customized models have been used in many marksmanship competitions. The Ruger 10/22 is currently available in. 22 Long Rifle, formerly available in. 22 Magnum and. 17 HMR. (10/22 Magnum and 10/17), but no longer. .22 Magnum models were discontinued in 2006 due to disappointing sales. As they did for the Mini-14, Sturm Ruger & Co. changed the 10/22 in 2005, offering a cheaper matte blued finish on the barrel and changing out the trigger guard from metal to plastic as well as a few other cosmetic differences.

The Ruger 10/22 has been used in covert military operations around the world, usually as a dedicated suppressed rifle. With subsonic ammunition, the suppressed shots from the 10/22 are essentially silent. The only sound you hear is the action racking back and forth. The IDF routinely carried suppressed and scoped Ruger 10/22s during the years of the "Intifada" in the West Bank and other places. The IDF snipers would "kneecap" the rioters' ringleaders from a distance, as crippling them was seen as preferable to killing them with conventional sniper rifles.

name: Ruger 22/45
description:
The Ruger 22/45 is a. 22 pistol manufactured by the Sturm, Ruger & Company. It is currently based on the Ruger Mk III but featured a polymer frame with a Colt 1911 style grip (some variants will accept 1911 grip plates). As with the more traditional Mk III, the 22/45 is available in a large number of different variants.

The Ruger 22/45 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ruger American Pistol
description:
The Ruger American Pistol (not to be confused with the Ruger American Rifle ), is an American polymer-framed pistol manufactured by Ruger. It fires via a pre-tensioned striker and uses a Browning-style locked-breech short recoil action. The American Pistol also features a unique barrel cam that lessens movements of the slide to the rear when a round is fired, resulting in less felt recoil. A compact variant was introduced in 2016 and a competition variant with a longer barrel, ported slide, adjustable target sights, and a pre-tapped mount for optics was introduced in 2020.

The Ruger American Pistol and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ruby pistol
description:
The Ruby pistol, also known as the Eibar-type pistol, was a type of pistol first developed in Spain by Spanish firm Gabilondo y Urresti (later Llama) in 1914, heavily based on the Browning Model 1903. It was adopted by the French Army in World War 1 as Pistolet Automatique de 7 millimètre 65 genre Ruby. From 1915-1918, Gabilondo mass-produced Ruby Pistols for the French military. The Ruby was issued to telephonists, stretcher bearers, Chauchat (machine gun) gunners along with loaders, 37mm mortar crews, and tank crews. It served in the French military until the 1930s (longer still in colonies like Indochina and Algeria). Ruby pistols were also supplied to Italy, Russia, Greece and Serbia, and after WW1 to Poland and Finland (from French stocks).

When Gabilondo was unable to keep up with the French demand for pistols, they subcontracted the contract to other production shops in Spain, who in turn subcontracted those to other even smaller workshops. This meant that the Ruby was produced by large amounts of manufacturers (about fifty) under a large amount of trade names, like Cebra, Liberty, Destroyer, et cetera. This caused issues with part interchangeability and even a casual comparison will show vast differences between manufacturers, but the French Military needed a lot of small arms fast, and therefore were in no position to argue this point.

name: Ruger Blackhawk
description:
The Ruger Blackhawk is a famed single action revolver first built by Ruger in the '50s. It is basically a Colt Single Action Army "Peacemaker" with a beefed up frame and cylinder, coiled springs, elevated and adjustable sights. Post-1973 models also incorporate a transfer bar, allowing the hammer to rest on a loaded cylinder without the risk of discharging the firearm, as well as simplified lockwork. It is sold in a vast array of calibers from. 30 Carbine, .41 Magnum, .45 Long Colt, .22 Long Rifle and. 22 Magnum, to the most popular, .44 Magnum and. 357 Magnum models.

Caliber: .30 Carbine, .32-20 Winchester/. 32 H&R Magnum Convertible, .327 Federal Magnum, .357 Magnum/9x19mm Parabellum Convertible, .357 Magnum, .357 Remington Maximum, .38-40 Winchester/10mm Auto Convertible, .41 Magnum, .44 Special, .44 Magnum, .45 Colt/. 45 Automatic Colt Pistol Convertible, .45 Colt

name: Ruger LCR
description:
The Ruger LCR (Lightweight Compact Revolver) is a polymer-framed revolver introduced by Ruger in 2009. It is available in. 22 LR, .22 WMR, 9x19mm Parabellum, .327 Federal Magnum, .38 Special +P or. 357 Magnum with factory options of Hogue Tamer, Hogue Boot or Crimson Trace Lasergrips, weighing 13 or 13. 5 ounces with the. 38 Special version and 17. 1 ounces in. 357 Magnum. It was an instant hit and popular for concealed carry. The. 357 Magnum version was offered in 2010 after positive consumer feedback on the revolver and Taurus and Smith & Wesson quickly followed with competing models.

Caliber: .38 Special, .357 Magnum, .22 LR, .22 WMR, .327 Federal Magnum, 9x19mm Parabellum

name: Ruger M77
description:
The Ruger M77 is a bolt-action rifle produced by Sturm, Ruger & Company. It was designed by Jim Sullivan during his three years with Ruger. The rifle features a traditional Mauser-style two-lugged bolt with a claw extractor.

Caliber: .30-06 Springfield, .223 Remington, .308 Winchester

name: Ruger LCP
description:
The Ruger LCP (Lightweight Compact Pistol) is a subcompact. 380 pistol that was unveiled by Ruger at the 2008 SHOT Show (and released in the summer of that same year), marking the company's first entry into the subcompact defensive handgun market. It has a 6+1 capacity and weighs less than 10 ounces.

The LCP's styling would be used in the 2011 locked-breech 9x19mm LC9, and its 2014 striker-fired variant, the LC9s. Both are near-identical externally (outside of scale - the 9x19mm variants are, naturally, slightly larger), making it difficult to tell them apart. The primary distinguishing features are the LCP's use of a button slide release on its left side (as both LC9 variants use a lever release) and the rectangular slot for the hammer in the rear of the slide (compared to the cross-shaped slot on the LC9, and no slot at all on the striker-fired LC9s). If fired, the LCP and LC9 can also be distinguished by the LCP's fixed barrel (as opposed to the Browning-Whiting-style tilting barrel seen in the 9x19mm variants). Most of the LC9's features would go on to appear in the LC380, a later variant (released in 2013) which is effectively an LC9 rechambered in. 380 ACP; as such, it holds seven rounds instead of the LCP's six, and uses the same locked-breech mechanism as its 9x19mm progenitors.

name: Ruger GP100
description:
The Ruger GP100 is an improvement on the Ruger Security Six line, featuring a thicker frame, modular handgrips, and a third locking lug at the front of the crane. The GP100 is available with a blued or stainless steel finish and a variety of calibers, barrel lengths, and barrel profiles. Originally the GP100 was fitted with distinctive rubber grips which had a rosewood inlay on either side. These were manufactured by Lett Grips and were made in standard and small sizes, with the small size generally fitted to the fixed sight 3 and 4 inch barrel models. Lett Grips went out of business in the late 90s (due in part to the death of the owner Bill Lett Sr. ) after which point Ruger began fitting rubber Hogue grips as standard or solid hardwood grips on some competition models. In 2016 Ruger began offering the old style grips as an aftermarket accessory, available through the company's on-line store.

Barrel Lengths: 2. 5 in (63 mm) 3" (76mm), 4" (102mm) and 6" (152mm)

name: Ruger Mk I / Mk II / Mk III Pistol
description:
The Ruger Mk I-IV series of pistols are affordable. 22 LR semiautomatic handguns developed by the Sturm, Ruger & Company in the years after World War II. The Mk I was Ruger's flagship handgun of the time, in Standard model and Mark I Target model, designed to penetrate the sports and recreational shooter market. Although bearing a strong resemblance to the German Luger P08 in layout and general appearance, the pistol is actually based upon a scaled down version of the Japanese Nambu Type 14.

The original Ruger Standard model (with tapered barrel) was in continuous production from 1949-1982. The heavy barreled Ruger Mk I Target was in production from 1951-1982. Each model of Ruger Pistol stopped production within one year of the introduction of the next generation of pistol. The newer marks introduced updated design features.

name: Ruger Model 44
description:
Ruger Model 44 is a semi auto hunting rifle, chambered in. 44 Magnum (hence the number of the model). It was produced from 1961 to 1985 and discontinued due to high production costs. Model 44 appeared under the name "Deerstalker" but this name was abandoned in 1962 due to a lawsuit by Ithaca Gun Company. The design of Model 44 influenced the Ruger 10/22 rifle.

The Ruger Model 44 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ruger Mini-14
description:
The Ruger Mini-14/AC-556 can be seen in the following movies and television series used by the following actors:

The Ruger Mini-14 is a semi-automatic rifle designed and manufactured by Ruger. It is one of the primary competitors to the AR-15 -style rifle on the civilian market. Introduced in 1973, the Mini-14 is so named because of its resemblance to the M14 Battle Rifle. Despite this, it is more mechanically similar to the M1 Garand with its self-cleaning, fixed-piston gas system. In 2003, the Mini-14 was upgraded with several functional and cost-saving features and thereafter, the Mini-14 name became used to describe a family of weapons rather than a sole rifle design.

name: Ruger Red Label
description:
The Ruger Red Label is an American over & under double barrelled shotgun of various calibers.

The Ruger Red Label and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Barrel Length: 26 in (66 cm) (12 gauge) | 28 in (71 cm) (20 gauge) | 29. 9 in (76 cm) (28 gauge)

name: Ruger Police Carbine
description:
The Ruger Police Carbine is an American semi-automatic carbine, an early example of a dedicated pistol-caliber carbine. It was released by Ruger in 1996 as a companion to the Ruger P-series pistols. The Police Carbine uses many of the same factory magazines utilized by P-series pistols and was available in 9x19mm Parabellum and. 40 S&W. A. 45 ACP version was tested, but never mass produced. It heavily resembles, and is in essence, a heavier-duty Ruger 10/22. Ruger conceived the Police Carbine as a service rifle for police officers on patrol (hence the name), with the idea being an officer can carry a Ruger pistol as a sidearm and use the Police Carbine if a situation called for a longer-ranged and more stable weapon. The main advantage of such a system would be the need to only carry one kind of magazine on their person. The Police Carbine was commercially unsuccessful and discontinued in 2006. While well-built and robust, the Police Carbine was unable to compete with lower cost pistol-caliber carbines like the Hi-Point Carbine or Kel-Tec SUB-2000.

A simple blowback-operated rifle, the Police Carbine is noted for its unusually large bolt meant to handle the pressures of firing 9x19mm or. 40 S&W via blowback. The bolt is actually two separate pieces- the main piece is lightweight and located in the receiver, while a weight sits in the foreend. Both pieces are joined by steel rods, allowing the weight of the massive bolt to be evenly distributed.

specs.Feed System: SR-series magazine, Security-9 magazine, Ruger American pistol magazine, or Glock magazine

name: Ruger Old Army
description:
The Ruger Old Army is a black-powder percussion revolver produced from 1972 to 2008 (the stainless model from 1975 to 2008). It isn't based on a historical design but is a modification of the Ruger Blackhawk, although it takes its styling cues from the Remington 1858 New Army.

Type: Muzzleloading, cap and ball, black powder revolver

name: Ruger Precision Rifle
description:
A purpose-built long distance shooting bolt-action rifle by Ruger, the Ruger Precision Rifle reuses many components common to the AR-15 family to reduce costs and increase customization.

The Ruger Precision Rifle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Calibers: .308 Winchester, 6. 5x49mm Creedmoor, 6x49mm Creedmoor, 6. 5x51. 6mm PRC, 6mm GT, .338 Lapua Magnum, .300 Winchester Magnum, .300 PRC

name: Ruger MP9
description:
The Ruger MP9 is an American 9x19mm submachine gun introduced in 1995. It was created by the famous Uziel Gal and is similar to the Uzi, his most famous creation. The MP9 uses a telescoping bolt, but fires from a closed bolt. The gun also has a quickly detachable barrel that is spring-loaded to cushion the impact of the bolt on closure. Despite incorporating several design improvement and being recognized as an improvement over the Uzi, the weapon that inspired it, the MP9 was a commercial failure and never attracted attention from any government agencies, its target market. Approximately 1, 500 were produced for only a single year and marked Ruger's first and, so far, only foray into the submachine gun market. A small number were also sold to movie armorers in Canada and Europe and have become common in film and television productions since the mid-1990s.

Type: Submachine Gun/Machine pistol

name: Ruger Redhawk / Super Redhawk
description:
The Ruger Redhawk is a large frame double action revolver, based on Ruger's Security Six line. Available in both blued and stainless steel finish, the Redhawk is distinguishable from other revolvers due to its intentionally manufactured reinforcements in the frame, chamber and cylinder, making it capable of taking very hot custom hand loads. This design feature was done to cater to the custom loaders, ever searching for more powerful handgun cartridges. At the time of its introduction, 1979, it was the most powerful handgun in the world, being that it could take the. 44 Magnum cartridge, custom loaded to maximize velocity. Such 'hot' loads were usually reserved for rifles and not recommended for any of the other. 44 Magnum handguns at the time, including the venerable Smith & Wesson Model 29 / 629. Since then other larger calibers and handguns have been introduced.

In the mid-1980s, some Redhawks had barrels fall off during shooting. Ruger developed an improved version of the Redhawk, dubbed the Super Redhawk, based on the GP-100 revolver. The Super Redhawk uses a modular grip frame and the frame extends out an additional 2 1/2" to allow for the mounting of a scope and newer, more powerful cartridges. After an investigation, Ruger realized that the issue with the barrels on the classic Redhawk existed only in a single batch that was using incorrect lubricant on the barrel threads. Rather than discontinuing the Redhawk, Ruger decided to continue building both designs and still produces the venerable Redhawk to this day. Since its introduction, Ruger has introduced newer calibers as well, like. 45 ACP, .45 Long Colt and. 357 Magnum to the classic Redhawk line, as well as re-introducing the older. 41 Magnum Redhawk as a Davidson's Distributor exclusive. The Super Redhawk line now includes other powerful chamberings such as. 454 Casull, .480 Ruger and 10mm Auto.

name: Ruger Security Six
description:
The Ruger Security Six, Service Six and Speed Six revolvers were the company's flagship double action revolver line. Introduced in 1969 at the NRA annual convention and released for commercial sales (after several engineering "tweaks") in 1971. Production ended in 1987 to make way for Ruger's newer line of handguns, the SP101, the GP100 and the Redhawk.

Barrel Lengths: 2. 75" (70mm), 4" (102 mm) & 6" (152 mm)

name: Ruger P-series pistol
description:
The Ruger P-series pistols can be seen in the following films, television series, and anime used by the following actors:

Caliber: 9x19mm, .40 S&W, .45 ACP, 7. 65x21mm (limited run of 5, 750 P89 barrels in 1994 only)

name: Ruger Security-9
description: The Ruger Security-9 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ruger Single Six / Ruger Vaquero
description:
Riding the wave of cowboy adventures and shows in the then 'new media' of television in the 1950s, Sturm Ruger Co. introduced a series of Single Action Army revolvers similar to the original Colt series. They initially created the Ruger Single Six which was a single action revolver outwardly similar to the Colts, however, the interiors were built like 20th century revolvers, thus cutting production costs. The later Ruger Single Six Revolvers has adjustable sights and was technically the inspiration for the later Blackhawk revolvers. In the early 1990s, Ruger introduced a fixed sight version of their cowboy style six shooter, called the Vaquero. The primary trait that distinguishes the more modern Vaqueros from the post-1960s Single Six revolvers is the fixed rear sight.

Like other SAA manufacturers, the Ruger revolver is sometimes used as a Colt impersonator in films and television shows. Though it is extremely close to the Colt Single Action Army, the Ruger has slight design elements that distinguish it from the Colt, most obviously the Ruger logo-marked grips. Other notable differences are 2 pins on the frame versus the SAA's 3, and a transfer bar instead of the SAA's hammer-mounted fixed firing pin.

name: Ruger-5.7
description:
The Ruger-5. 7 is a 5. 7x28mm pistol developed by Ruger.

The Ruger-5. 7 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Röhm Revolvers
description:
Röhm revolvers are a series of (primarily. 22 caliber) revolvers manufactured by Röhm GmbH in Sontheim, Germany in the 1960s. They are notorious for their poor quality craftmanship and for their low pricing; less than $50 for most models. The most widely known model is the RG-14, which is infamous for being used by John Hinckley Jr. in his attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan in 1981.

Röhm revolvers can be seen in the following films and television series used by the following actors:

name: S-Mine
description:
The Schrapnellmine is perhaps the most well-known bounding antipersonnel mine. Developed in the 1930s by Nazi Germany, the mine consists of an outer casing surrounding the mine body, with a propellant charge underneath. When triggered, either by applying pressure to the prongs, operating a tripwire rigged to them, or by any other type of fuze that might be substituted, the propellant charge is set off, throwing the body 3-5 feet vertically whereupon the main charge detonates, creating a horizontal spread of shrapnel with a lethal radius of around 22 yards and a casualty radius of around 150 yards. This earned the mine the nickname Bouncing Betty among American troops, though this nickname has since often been applied to any bounding antipersonnel mine, not just the S-Mine. Jumping Jack was also used by Australian and New Zealand soldiers, while the French called it "The Silent Soldier".

S-Mine dischargers were also mounted in brackets on the hulls of early production Wehrmacht armoured vehicles, used for close-range anti-infantry defence; early versions of the Tiger I had five such devices. These were essentially small mortars that fired the mine's bounding body as a projectile.

name: Ruger SP101
description:
The SP101 is a double-action revolver manufactured by Ruger. It is a small frame all-steel revolver, with a five-shot (. 38 Special, .357 Magnum, and 9×19mm Parabellum), six-shot (. 327 Federal Magnum and. 32 H&R Magnum), or eight-shot (. 22 LR) cylinder. The SP101 was introduced in 1989 as the smaller-frame counterpart to the GP100. It is also notable for being the company's first and only small frame revolver until the Ruger LCR was introduced in 2009.

Barrel Lengths: 2. 25" (57. 15 mm), 3. 125" (77. 79 mm), 4" (101. 6 mm)

name: Ruger SR9
description: The Ruger SR9 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Sa 23 submachine gun series
description:
The Sa 23 (Czech: Samopal 23 ) series of submachine guns was first conceived just after World War II, when Czech arms designer Vaclav Holek developed a submachine gun patterned after the various conventional blowback designs of the time (the MP 40, Sten, etc. ), but with some innovative updates. The resultant weapon was the first submachine gun in full production to have the magazine well in the pistol grip, and a wrap around bolt.

The development of this SMG was started in 1947 at the Česká Zbrojovka Strakonice arms factory, as the CZ-447. The final version of the SMG was adopted by the Czechoslovak army in August 1948 under the designation "9 mm samopal vz. 48a" (with fixed wooden buttstock) and "9 mm samopal vz. 48b" (with folding buttstock). Serial production was started in 1949 at the Česká Zbrojovka enterprise - Uhersky Brod. In the spring of 1950, the Czechoslovak army changed the designation of these SMGs: Sa vz. 48a became Sa 23, and Sa vz. 48b became Sa 25. Unlike other Czechoslovakian submachine guns, these numeral designations are typically written without the "vzor" (model of), as their numeral designations are not their years of introductions.

name: SA-14 Gremlin
description:
SA-14 Gremlin is the NATO reporting name of the 9K34 Strela-3, a Russian man portable surface to air missile. It is heavily based on the SA-7 Grail (9K32 Strela-2) to the point where it is nearly identical in appearance: the main feature that differentiates the two is that the SA-14 has a spherical battery coolant unit (BCU) at the front of the gripstock under the launch tube's muzzle, while the SA-7 has a cylindrical unit with a plunger-like structure extending from the front, which is just a thermal battery. The BCU contains not just a thermal battery, but also a supply of liquefied gas (liquid nitrogen, as opposed to the more inert but harder to manufacture liquid argon used by the FIM-92 Stinger ) which is used to cool the missile's IR seeker to operating temperature prior to launch.

The naval variant is referred to by the NATO designation SA-N-8.

name: SA-7 Grail
description:
SA-7 Grail is the NATO reporting name of the Russian MANPADS 9K32 Strela-2, a shoulder mounted, man portable anti aircraft missile launcher widely exported to Warsaw Pact countries and is comparable to the FIM-43 Redeye of NATO origin.

The SA-7 Grail and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Sa vz. 58
description:
The Sa vz. 58 ( Samopal vzor 58; "Submachine gun model 58") is a Czechoslovakian assault rifle designed in the 1950s. Although externally somewhat similar to the AK-47 series of rifles, it is actually a much different weapon, with the internals being closer to those of the German StG 44 (with some differences). Despite firing the same cartridge (7. 62x39mm) as the AK-47 variants, no vz. 58 part, including the magazine, is interchangeable with those of the AK-47. Though the term "Sa" (which is short for Samopal, which means "submachine gun") is part of this firearm's official name, it is not a submachine gun as most firearms historians use the term. "Sa" was used for all subsequent select fire weapons that Czechoslovakia built and adopted after their last indigenous design, the vz. 52 semi-automatic rifle.

The vz. 58 was designed in 1956, in response to Soviet pressure to have all Warsaw Pact nations use the same common ammunition types, in this case 7. 62x39mm. It entered service in 1959, and over 920, 000 were manufactured until 1984. Its first proposed replacement was the unsuccessful CZ 2000 assault rifle, which was shelved in 2007. It has been since succeeded in Czech service by the CZ 805 BREN series. Besides use by Czechoslovakia (and later Czechia and Slovakia), the vz. 58 series was widely exported worldwide, and has been used in conflicts like the Vietnam War, Nigerian Civil War, The Troubles, South African Border War, Gulf War, Iraq War, Syrian Civil War, and Russo-Ukrainian War.

specs.Length: 25. 4 in (64. 5 cm) with stock extended, 17. 1 in (43. 5 cm) with stock folded

name: Sa. 81 Krása
description:
The Sa 81 Krása (Czech for ‘beauty’) was a carbine developed by Jiří Čermák, in the late 1970s. The Czechoslovakian Army requested a weapon suitable for use by rear-echelon troops and vehicle crews. Beside the 7, 62x39mm version, a 5, 45x39mm concept was planned as the Sa. 83, before being scrapped, with the prototype being donated to the Military History Institute in Prague.

The Sa. 81 Krása and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Length: 22. 1 in (56. 2 cm) (stock extended), 12. 4 in (31. 5 cm) (stock folded)

name: Saab Bofors Dynamics CBJ-MS
description:
Calibers: 6. 5x25 CBJ-MS, 9x19mm

Weight (w/o magazine): 6. 17 lbs (2. 8 kg)

name: SA-18 Grouse
description:
SA-18 Grouse is the NATO reporting name of the Russian MANPADS 9K38 Igla (Russian: Игла́, "needle"), a highly improved and upgraded version of the SA-7 Grail and is the current shoulder-launched SAM (Rus. ПЗРК) for the Russian and former Eastern bloc armies.

The SA-18 Grouse and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Saab Bofors Dynamics AT4
description:
Not to be confused with the AT-4 Spigot

The Saab Bofors Dynamics AT4 is a Swedish-made single-shot 84mm unguided light anti-tank recoilless launcher system, effectively a single-shot Carl Gustav Recoilless Rifle: in spite of what its own US Army field manual claims (due to copy-pasting large blocks of text from the manual for the M72 LAW ), it is not a rocket launcher, as the round does not have an integral motor. It was originally developed by Förenade Fabriksverken (FFV): FFV Ordnance was later merged with Bofors AB as part of the Celsius Group, which was bought up by Saab AB in 1999: while Saab would later sell the heavy weapons division of Bofors to United Defense, Saab retained its missile and rocket division.

specs.Years of Use: 1984 - present (baseline M136 AT4 no longer procured as of ~2010
specs.Weight: 14. 8 lbs (6. 7 kg) (M136 AT4, AT4CS AST), ~15. 4 lbs (7 kg) (AT4 HEAT, current production), ~17. 6 lbs (8 kg) (AT4CS HP, AT4CS RS), ~19. 8 lbs (9 kg) (AT4CS ER, AT4CS HE)
specs.Length: 40. 2 in (102 cm) (M136 AT4), "less than 39. 4 in (100 cm)" (all modern variants)

name: SA-25
description:
SA-25 is the NATO reporting name of the Russian MANPADS 9K333 Verba.

The weapon is not to be confused with the Sa 25 submachine gun.

specs.Feed System: 1- to 6-shot fixed box magazine (varies with individual magazine capacity)

name: Saab Bofors Dynamics RBS 70
description:
The Saab Bofors Dynamics RBS 70 ("Robotsystem 70, " since "robot" can mean "missile" in Swedish) is surface-to-air missile system which was designed to replace the MIM-23 Hawk SAM system, FIM-43 Redeye and Bofors 40mm guns in Swedish air defense service. It is often classified as a MANPADS, though it is the heaviest system called such, requiring a three- to four-man crew to transport it. It resembles an ATGM launcher, with a large stand and sight unit which missiles in disposable casings are attached to.

The variable crew is due to the modular nature of the system, which breaks down into pack loads: only one man is actually required to operate the assembled launcher. For transport, at minimum one crewman carries the main body of the system, which consists of the gyrostabilizer sight and power supply. A second carries the stand, and a third carries encased missiles. There are three additional optional modules: the Raytheon Cossor IFF880 IFF antenna and its associated equipment, the Saab Vectronics COND (Clip-On Night Device) which is an IR night vision system which attaches to the front of the sight unit, and a data terminal which allows the RBS 70 to coordinate with Giraffe fire control radars. One Giraffe can coordinate with up to nine launchers.

name: Sabatti ST-18
description: The Sabatti ST-18 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Sage Control Ordnance Deuce
description: The Sage Control Ordnance Deuce and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

Sage Control Ordnance, in addition to breach loading single shot launchers, also manufacture 6 shot and 8 shot rotary launchers with fixed and folding stocks as well as offered in non-restricted 37mm gas grenade Law Enforcement versions and 40mm Destructive Device Versions.

The Sage Control Rotary Launchers and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Saiga rifle series
description:
The Saiga rifle series is a family of Russian AK-derived civilian hunting rifles.

The Saiga rifle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Saiga shotgun series
description:
The Saiga shotgun series is a series of AK -derived civilian semi-automatic shotguns developed and manufactured by Izhmash (now known as Kalashnikov Concern ). The Saiga is primarily manufactured in three different calibers: 12 gauge (Saiga 12 series), 20 gauge (Saiga 20 series), and. 410 bore (Saiga. 410 series).

Each of these three types of Saiga shotguns generally have three main variants:

name: Sako 75
description:
The Sako 75 rifle was introduced in 1997, on the company's 75th anniversary (hence the designation). It was produced till the mid-2000s, when it was superseded by the Sako 85.

The Sako 75 rifle can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Sako TRG
description:
The Sako TRG (short for Finnish: "Tarkkuuskivääri Riihimäki G-sarja", Finnish for "Riihimäki Precision Rifle G-series", with Riihimaki being the city of production and G representing "7", as the TRG was developed as a successor of the Sako TR-6), is a Finnish bolt-action sniper rifle, made by Sako and derived from the Valmet M86. The TRG is considered one of the best sniper rifles in the world and widely adopted among various special forces.

The Sako TRG sniper rifle and variants appears in the following:

name: SAR-80
description:
The SAR-80 is a very close design copy of the AR-18. The weapon visually and internally resembles an AR-18, although the furniture, rear sight, receiver shape and finish are different. Also, unlike the AR-18, the standard SAR-80 has a fixed buttstock, although some side-folding models were made. One other improvement over the AR-18 is that the SAR-80 was designed to accept STANAG M16 magazines. The SAR-80 was actually designed by Frank Waters of the British company Sterling in 1980. Sterling had a license to manufacture the AR-18 but could not license the design to other countries. Sterling was, however, able to make a new copy of the AR-18 and then sold the design to Singapore, which locally manufactured the SAR-80 for mostly non-front line soldiers like logistics units. (The SAF prefered the M16S1, a locally licensed-produced M16A1 owing to the SAR 80 not having any provision for low-light sights and the earlier models having serious quality issues) and for export purposes. The SAR-80 was also adopted by various other militaries, including the Central African Republic’s Gendarmerie, the Croatian Army, the Papua New Guinea Defence Force and the Slovenian Territorial Army.

The SAR-80 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Saritch
description: The Saritch (Russian: Сарыч, Sarych, "common buzzard") is a Russian concept bullpup assault rifle. It was designed by design school student Andrei Ovsyannikov as a part of his graduation design project. No functional firing models were made. It was designed as a modular civilian carbine (and the mock-up is made as one) with semi-automatic fire and 10-round magazines, but military versions with compact barrels, 30-round magazines and automatic fire were conceived by the designer.

name: Sako 85
description: The Sako 85 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Salient Arms International GRY
description:
The Salient Arms International GRY is an AR-15 type rifle by SAI. Its most distinctive feature is its JailBrake muzzle brake.

The Salient Arms International GRY and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:
