name: Taurus PT92
description:
The Taurus PT92 (and the similar PT99 ) is a Brazilian-made "Wonder Nine" based on the original Beretta 92. The PT92's story dates back to 1974, when Beretta won a contract to supply the Brazilian military with pistols. They built a factory in Sao Paulo and operated it until 1980, when they sold it to Taurus, who then started making very early original Taurus PT92s, as almost exact copies of the Beretta 92 (as the patents had expired), featuring the non-ambidextrous safety, round trigger guard and, most notably, the heel-mounted magazine releases and shiny plastic grips, but this design was quickly replaced by the current magazine catch location. Nonetheless, magazines for the Taurus PT92 often have cuts for both magazine releases. Despite the many design changes, such as the redesigned slide serrations, thickening of the trigger guard hook and addition of an accessory rail, the Taurus PT92 still retains many of the design elements from the original Beretta 92, such as the shape of the trigger and the location of the safety.

In many action movies (particularly those made in Hong Kong), the Taurus PT92/PT99 has been used as a stand-in for the Beretta 92F/FS when the Beretta is not available. Thus, the two guns are very often confused by moviegoers. The Taurus is distinguished from the Beretta by its frame-mounted safety, which it retains from the original Beretta 92, whereas the 92F/FS has its safety mounted on the slide. Even from a distance, this makes the two guns easy to tell apart. Newer-model PT92s and PT99s can also be told apart from the 92F/FS because the cocking serrations at the rear of the slide are wider than those of the 92F/FS (in order to improve grip on the slide while a round is being chambered). Both PT92s and PT99s now come standard with accessory rails since 2005.

name: Techno Arms MAG-7
description:
The Techno Arms MAG-7 is a rather bizarre South African pump-action combat shotgun. Resembling an extremely beefy Uzi (it is only slightly smaller than an M1A1 Carbine with the stock folded), the weapon fires special 60mm (2. 3622 inch) length 12-gauge shells from a detachable magazine about the size of a 30-round 5. 56mm STANAG which is inserted into the pistol grip: as a result the grip is extremely thick.

The basic version sold in South Africa either had no stock at all or an Armsel Striker -style folding stock, but to comply with US import laws the MAG-7M1 was developed. This variant mounted an incredibly dorky-looking fixed wooden stock along with an extension to increase the barrel length to the required 18 inches. Techno also made an even stranger variant, the "dual riot, " which mounts a 37mm top-break flare launcher on top of the MAG-7 and has an odd, vaguely futuristic-looking stock.

specs.Caliber: Proprietary 2. 3622 inch (60mm) length 12-gauge (can chamber various 12-gauge mini shells but will not function reliably)

name: Tellermine 42
description:
The Tellermine 42 is a German World War II anti-tank mine. It was basically an improved Tellermine 35 (Stahl). The most visible difference between the two models is the smaller diameter of the top cover which was made to ensure the detonation below Soviet T-34 and KV-1 tanks. A further improvement was the Tellermine (Pilz) 43 which was introduced in March 1943.

The Tellermine 42 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Tellermine 35
description:
The Tellermine 35 is a German World War II anti-tank mine. Due to the low activation weight (100-190kg), it often detonated too early when touching the tracks of tanks which meant it could not utilize the full explosion effect on the target. To fix this problem, the improved Tellermine 35 (Stahl) was invented which increased the activation weight from 240kg to 300kg. When German forces first faced the heavier tanks of the Soviet Union, an even more advanced version, the Tellermine 42, was introduced in 1942.

The Tellermine 35 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ten-X Ammunition TX-12 Special Task Weapon
TDI Vector
description:
The Vector is a series of weapons developed by KRISS USA (formerly known as TDI, Transformational Defense Industries). They use diverted recoil in the form of the "Kriss Super V" system, in which an inertia block travels downwards in a recess behind the magwell as the action cycles in an attempt to offset muzzle climb (the fact that it then has to travel back upwards means this actually just creates an odd up-down oscillating motion when firing in auto). The action was patented by French designer Renaud Kerbrat, formerly of FN Herstal, while the polymer outer housing and folding stock of the weapon were designed by Magpul.

There are four main variants marketed: the SMG is the military and Law Enforcement version with a 5. 5 inch barrel and select-fire. The civilian semi-auto version of the SMG is called the SBR ("short barrel rifle"). For states with SBR bans, the CRB ("carbine") version is available with a 11. 5-inch permanent extension to the standard barrel - oddly referred to as a "safety extension" in some literature. A "pistol" version with an end cap instead of a stock is also available: the SDP ("Special Duty Pistol" or "Security Detail Pistol"). All civilian models used to have "/SO" ("Special Operations") appended to their designation, though KRISS USA has since given up on doing this.

specs.Type: Submachine Gun (Vector SMG), Semi-Auto Carbine (Vector SBR and CRB), Pistol (Vector SDP)
specs.Caliber: .45 ACP, 9x19mm (announced 2015, using an alternate lower). 40 S&W (K10 and recent Vectors), 9x21mm IMI, .357 SIG, 10mm Auto, .22 LR
specs.Weight: 5. 6 lbs (2. 5 kg) (SMG and SBR), 7 lb (3. 2 kg) (CRB)
specs.Length: 24. 3 in (62 cm) (SMG and SBR, stock extended), 34. 8 in (88 cm) (CRB)
specs.Barrel length: 5. 5 in (14 cm) (SMG and SBR), 6. 5 in (16. 5 cm) (SMG and SBR Gen II, optional), 8 in (20. 3 cm) (export SBR only available in Ukraine), 16 in (40. 6 cm) (CRB, permanently affixed 11. 5 in extension to the 5. 5 in barrel), 18. 6 in (47. 2 cm) (Canadian CRB)
specs.Capacity: . 45 ACP: Glock 21-style magwell, usual capacities 10, 13. The optional MagEx kit was originally marketed as 30 rounds, and "25+" afterwards. The current MagEx2 kit extends the capacity to 30. | 9x19mm Parabellum & 9x21mm IMI: Glock 17-style magwell, usual capacities 10, 17, 19, 33. The MagEx2 9x19mm capacity is 40. | . 40 S&W: Glock 22-style magwell, usual capacities 10, 15, 22. | 10mm Auto: Glock 20-style magwell, usual capacities 10, 15. The MagEx2 capacity is 33. | . 357 SIG: Glock 31-style magwell, usual capacities 10, 15. | . 22 LR: 10, 30.
specs.Fire Modes: Semi-Auto / 2-Round Burst / Full-Auto; civilian versions are restricted to Semi-Auto.

name: Textron NGSW-R
description:
The Textron NGSW-R is an American battle rifle designed by Textron, developed for the US military's Next Generation Squad Weapon program (specifically the NGSW-R requirement for a new rifle; a companion machine gun, the NGSW-AR, was likewise developed). The rifle was unveiled in 2019, and went through multiple iterations, competing against the LoneStar Future Weapons RM277 and SIG Sauer MCX-SPEAR before ultimately losing out to the latter in 2022.

The rifle uses a proprietary 6. 8mm cased-telescoped round (derived from Textron's earlier work on the LSAT program), along with a gas-operated vertically-moving-chamber system similar to the Steyr ACR; the blocky assembly at the bottom of the handguard is actually the ejection chute, with the weapon ejecting spent cases out the left side of this assembly. As with the other NGSW-R entrants, the Textron rifle is meant to be used in conjunction with a proprietary suppressor.

name: TD Multi Port Plus Distraction Device
description:
The TD Multi Port Plus Distraction Device is a rechargeable flashbang grenade produced by American company Defense Technology. It is considered to have the most powerful pressure wave among flashbang grenades because of its contents: 15 grams of flash powder which offer the dynamic explosive content, producing a report and pressure wave stronger than other flashbangs.

The TD Multi Port Plus Distraction Device and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Thompson Submachine Gun
description:
The legendary Thompson Submachine Gun is an American SMG developed in the late 1910s by General John T Thompson. Initially envisioning an "auto rifle" to replace bolt-action rifles in American service while using an operating system less expensive than recoil or gas, Thompson acquired a patent issued to John Bell Blish for what turned out to be a friction-delayed blowback action. However, since the only cartridge in US military service that would work with the Blish action was the. 45 ACP pistol round, Thompson changed his goal to a "one-man, hand-held machine gun, " imagining it being used as a "trench broom. "

The earliest design from autumn of 1917 was a bizarre belt-fed weapon called the "Persuader. " The resulting prototype was very similar to the original sketch (with the only exception that the intended fancy Lewis gun -style radiator cooling system (minus the top cover) was replaced during implementation with a conventional tubular metal casing). However, in practice, the resulting system turned out to be extremely unreliable, which prevented its expected rapid adaptation by the military. As a result of further improvements and research, this morphed into the magazine-fed "Annihilator" in 1918-1919, which boasted a staggering fire rate of up to 1, 500 RPM, rendering the weapon virtually uncontrollable in fullauto. A pattern gun with no stock or sights was presented to Colt later on in 1919 to be redesigned for production. This became the M1921 and was marketed as a "submachine gun: " while not the first weapon of the type, it was the first to actually use the name.

name: Tingle Model 1960
description: The Tingle Model 1960 is a single-shot, muzzle loaded in-line black powder pistol and with the hammer acting as a half-cock safety. Developed by Bob Tingle, a World War II veteran who set up an all-purpose blacksmith and welding shop just outside the town of Shelbyville in east central Indiana. However, the rights would be sold to Armi San Marco.

name: TiSAS Zigana
description:
The TiSAS Zigana pistol is manufactured by the Turkish firearms manufacturer Trabzon Silah Sanayi AŞ (TiSAS) and based on the German Walther P88 Compact. The Zigana is available in a large number of variants and calibers (9x19mm being the standard), including the original M16 service pistol, long barrel, compact, and accurized sport variants. It is offered with satin chrome, black, or two tone finishes. A version of the pistol with an underbarrel rail and vented slide (but not frame) is imported into the US by American Tactical Imports as the CS9/FS9 (Compact/Fullsize respectively) and is available in sport (compensated barrel and target sights) and standard versions.

Caliber: 9x19mm, 9x21mm, .40 S&W (C40) and. 45 ACP (C45)

name: TKB-0111
description:
The TKB-0111 (Тульское Конструкторское Бюро, образец № 0111; Tula Design Bureau, model number 0111) - is an experimental prototype Soviet assault rifle, developed in the early 70s by G. A. Korobov at TsKIB SOO to participate in the competition of the USSR Ministry of Defense for a new assault rifle for the Soviet Army ("OKR Abakan"). According to the results of this competition, TKB-0111 received a bad mark and, as a result, the Nikonov assault rifle won this competition.

Action is based on the gas-operated reloading, locking was performed by rotating bolt. The barrel is equipped with a gas-dynamic compensator. The reloading handle and the fire selector are located on the right side of the receiver.

name: TKB-011
description:
The TKB-011 (Тульское Конструкторское Бюро, образец № 011; Tula Design Bureau, model number 011) is a line of experimental Soviet assault rifles, developed by Nikolai M. Afanasyev in 1963. The externals of the rifles are made of bakelite. The TKB-011 and TKB-022 were the first to use a tunnel type ejection port, which is now used on the FN F2000.

The TKB-011 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

description:
The TKB-0146 is a prototype Soviet assault rifle designed by Igor Stechkin. It was created for the Project Abakan trials (as with the AN-94 ) to replace the AK-74. Of note it was more accurate but less reliable and more complicated than the AN-94.

One of the early models fired in three-round bursts instead of two.

name: TKB-408
description:
The TKB-408 (Тульское Конструкторское Бюро, образец № 408; Tula Design Bureau, model number 408) - is an experimental prototype of a Soviet assault rifle, developed in 1946 by G. A. Korobov at TsKIB SOO to participate in the competition for a new assault rifle for the Soviet Army. According to the results of this competition, TKB-408 showed unsatisfactory results in both accuracy of fire and survivability, and dropped out of the competition after only 5000 shots were fired. As a result, the famous 7. 62-mm Kalashnikov assault rifle won this competition.

Action is based on the gas-operated reloading, locking was performed by rotating bolt. The gas piston and recoil spring are located above the barrel. The ejection port is located on the right, there is a ejection port cover that folds down. The cocking handle is located above the forend on the left. The barrel is equipped with a compensator. The fire selector is located on the left side of the receiver above the pistol grip. The rifle uses special 30-round magazines, which have special protrusions-hooks, for attachment to the latch located at the bottom of the pistol grip.

name: TM-35
description:
The TM-35 is a Soviet Union (Russian) World War II Anti-tank mine, there were basically 2 different versions, the TM-35 and the improved TM-35M which were very similar.

The TM-35 was the initial version which was introduced into service in 1935. It was a rectangular, metal cased anti tank mine which featured a carrying handle on one side. The detonation charge consisted of several 200g TNT blocks. In 1939 an improved version, the TM-35M was introduced which featured a larger detonation charge of 4kg. This increased the overall height of the mine to 115mm. When World War II started, production of the earlier TM-35 ended but available supplies were used up. Both versions served in Soviet service until the end of WWII.

name: Tokarev AT-44
description:
The Tokarev AT-44 (Russian: Автомат Токарева АТ-44, lit. "Tokarev assault rifle AT-44" ) is a Soviet prototype assault rifle based on the SVT-40. In order to develop infantry weapons chambered for the new 7. 62x41mm cartridge, many projects from various organizations were launched, with famed firearms designer Fedor Vasilievich Tokarev deciding to experiment with a new assault rifle design. At this time, Tokarev no longer worked in Tula, having been evacuated together with part of the engineering and technical staff of TsKB-14 in February 1943 to Moscow's OKB-16 design bureau (now A. E. Nudelman Design Bureau "Tokhmash") Tokarev started design on a new rifle in October 1943, and on November 21 of the same year, the model was ready. Tokarev did not create a completely new design, instead modifying his earlier AVT-40 rifle, which was in mass production. This approach allowed Tokarev to reuse much of the design principles from the AVT-40 and shorten the time needed to work on the prototype. The weapon is virtually identical to the AVT-40 mechanically. It is gas operated with a shorter receiver and redesigned trigger group in order to accommodate the addition of a pistol grip, and also features an integrated bipod. The fire selector is a simple lever located on the trigger guard itself.

Several prototypes were built, but like the AVT-40 that preceded it, the AT-44 proved to be rather inadequate as an automatic weapon. It was swiftly rejected after initial tests- once again proving the SVT-40 design was better left as a semi-automatic only rifle.

name: Tokyo Arsenal Experimental Submachine Gun
description:
The Experimental Submachine Gun (Japanese: 試製自動短銃, Shisei-jidō tanjū) was an early Japanese experimental submachine gun made by the Tokyo Arsenal. Its name is also attributed as Tokyo Arsenal Model 1927 and Tokyo Arsenal Model 1928, though no corresponding Japanese name appears to exist; it is likely this was coined by an English-speaking firearm historian to describe the weapon, which was made in 1927 / 1928 (Year 2 and 3 of Shōwa era). The weapon was rejected by the IJA due to its unreliability. Information on the weapon is limited, even in Japanese sources.

There are two weapons under this name. Namely Tokyo Arsenal Model 1927 and Tokyo Arsenal Model 1928. Both names are a simplification for Western observers to distinguish the two models. You can also call both weapons Tokyo Arsenal Model 1 and Tokyo Arsenal Model 2. It is worth remembering that the term:

specs.Capacity: 50-round tape feed through drum magazine / 30-round detachable stick magazine

name: Tokarev PPT
description:
Tokarev, like many other designers, did not stop at just one weapon design. The most famous design of this designer when it comes to submachine guns is the 1927 model, the PPT-27 chambered for 7. 62x38mmR Nagant ammunition. But in 1928 (PPT-28), Tokarev presented his next model. Which was an adaptation for 7. 63x25mm Mauser ammunition. The next model was the PPT-31, it was more simplified than its predecessor and more reliable. It already uses standard 7. 62x25mm ammunition.

Produced in very limited quantities. The first had a split grip and stock in the style of a Thompson, and the second received a cooling jacket, giving it some external resemblance to the PPD. Some M1928s and all M1931s are chambered in 7. 62x25mm Tokarev.

name: Tower Percussion Pistol
description:
The Tower Percussion pistol was used by the British prior to and after the Crimean war, as well as the Confederate States during the American Civil War. It was produced by the Tower of London Armories (that's why it got that name).

The Tower Percussion Pistol and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Tokarev SVT
description:
The SVT is a series of semi-automatic (later also automatic) rifles designed by Soviet firearm designer Fedor Vasilievich Tokarev to replace older models in the Soviet Army's equipment, namely the AVS-36. The entire line of weapons includes the following models:

The Tokarev SVT-38 - (RUS: Самозарядная Винтовка Токарева 1938 года / Samozaryadnayaa Vintovka Tokariewa 1938 goda | ENG: Tokarev self-loading rifle 1938) is a Soviet semi-auto battle rifle, a predecessor of much better known SVT-40. The SVT-38 was the penultimate version of a series of Tokarev designs (starting from М1921; and can trace it linage back to the M1933, the first rifle of such design, through the previous experimental models). This was adopted to complement the AVS-36, in the role of a semi-auto only rifle. The SVT-38 was produced for less than a year, and was replaced by the improved SVT-40.

name: TOZ .22 Caliber Rifle Series
description:
The Russian company Tulsky Oruzheiny Zavod (TOZ) manufactures. 22 caliber bolt action rifles from the 1920s until the present day. Early models were intended for basic shooting training, but they quickly became popular among hunters. The most widely produced models are single-shot TOZ-7, TOZ-8 (more than one million produced), TOZ-12, and magazine-fed TOZ-17 and TOZ-18. In the modern day, TOZ produces the TOZ-78 model.

The TOZ. 22 Caliber Rifles and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Tokarev TT-33
description:
The Tokarev TT-33 and variants have appeared in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

The TT-33 was introduced (as TT-30) in 1930 by Fedor Tokarev, who drew heavily from John Browning's FN Browning Model 1903 and Colt Model 1911 pistols. In 1933, the design was slightly changed, in particular, with an updated firing mechanism; the removable cover on the back of the grip (for quicker access to the trigger group) was also removed, making the frame one-piece, and the weapon more technologically advanced and less expensive to manufacture. The TT-30/33 was intended to replace the obsolete Nagant M1895 and a mix of various other revolvers and semi-auto pistols, which were in service, but production was never sufficient to fully replace the vast number of M1895 revolvers already in service and manufacture. The pistol is chambered in 7. 62x25mm Tokarev, which is based on the 7. 63x25mm Mauser round of the Mauser C96 (which had been widely used by Bolshevik forces during the Russian Civil War) but loaded to higher pressure levels.

TOZ-94: (1994 - 2012) TOZ-194: (1994 - 2006)

The TOZ-94 shotgun is a pump-action variant of the semi-automatic TOZ-87, introduced in 1994.

name: TOZ-36
description:
The TOZ-36 is a Soviet target revolver, designed by Yefim Khaidurov and manufactured from 1962 to 1977. It is partly based on the Nagant M1895, but otherwise it is a completely different weapon (in particular, also somewhat inspired by Smith and Wesson Masterpiece ), using its own ammunition (shooting from the TOZ-36 with a regular Nagant cartridge is strictly prohibited). The similar TOZ-49 uses the 7. 62x26R cartridge. Both revolvers were widely used in various sporting contests, including the Olympic Games. In 1996, nearly twenty years after the production was discontinued, a new version appeared: the TOZ-96, a version of TOZ-49 chambered in. 32 S&W Long.

The TOZ-36 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: TOZ-35
description:
The TOZ-35 is a Soviet single-shot target pistol designed by Yefim Khaidurov in the early 1960s. It has been widely exported and in the late 1970s, a modified version, the TOZ-35M, was introduced.

(1962 -? : TOZ-35, 1978 -? TOZ-35M)

specs.Weight: 39 oz (1. 1 kg) ~ 49 oz (1. 4 kg) (depending on type of grip)

name: TP-82
description:
The TP-82 (Russian: ТП-82) is a Soviet triple-barreled survival weapon issued to cosmonauts. The idea of a cosmonaut survival weapon was first experimented with by TsNIITochMash in the 70s, after Soviet cosmonaut Alexey Leonov approached TsNIITochMash and raised his concerns about the need of a survival weapon for hunting should a cosmonaut land in remote and inhospitable wilderness filled with wild animals. Leonov himself had the experience of landing in the middle of nowhere during the Voskhod 2 mission. The cosmonauts had access to a Portable Survival Kit (Носимый аварийный запас, Nosimyi Avariynyi Zapas), or NAZ, in their Soyuz capsule, but it only contained a Makarov PM that was unsuitable for hunting.

In 1981, it was decided that the SONAZ, or "Small Arms of Portable Survival Kit" (Russian: СОНАЗ, Стрелковое Оружие Носимого Аварийного Запаса ), would be put into full development, which would contain a weapon and its ammunition. One year after the project was announced, the TP-82 was developed, and was selected as the SONAZ.

specs.Length: 14. 2 in (36 cm); with stock: 26. 4 in (67 cm)

name: TOZ-87
description:
The TOZ-87 is a Soviet/Russian semi-auto gas-operated shotgun. Development began in 1978 as the "MTs 24-12". In the late 1980s, the MTs-24 competed against the TTs-22 (more known as the "second generation" MTs-22), another gas-operated shotgun, for the role of a new mass-produced semi-automatic shotgun. The MTs-24 was declared the winner, and TOZ began producing the gun in 1990 under the TOZ-87 index, with the transition to mass production in 1993 (the TTs-22 was produced in small quantities from 1989). A "Cruiser" version, the TOZ-187, was also created. It is distinguished by the factory pistol grip (with the possibility of attaching a folding stock) and shorter barrel.

The TOZ-94, a pump-action version of the TOZ-87, was developed in 1994.

name: Trailblazer Firearms LifeCard
description:
The Trailblazer Firearms LifeCard is a foldable single-shot break-action pistol produced by Trailblazer Firearms. The name is derived from its dimensions; when folded, the LifeCard is roughly the size (barring its width) of a typical credit card. Barrels are available in. 22 LR and. 22 WMR, either with or without threading, with a compartment for spare rounds being present in the grip. The pistol is single-action only, with the striker (the small rectangular component at the rear of the upper section) having to be manually cocked before firing; it also lacks sights of any sort (barring a simple groove along the top of the frame and barrel), being intended exclusively for short-range use.

The Trailblazer Firearms LifeCard and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Tromboncino M28
description: The Tromboncino Modello 28 was an Italian attempt to develop a grenade launcher, designed to be used with the Carcano M91 TS (Truppe Speciali). Its name means little trumpet in Italian. The trigger unit is linked to the trigger of the carbine. When loaded the host carbine remains inoperable because its bolt is used in the launcher, and its trigger operates both sears (in carbine and in the launcher) at the same time.

name: Triple Action Thunder
description:
The Thunder is a prototype breech-loaded single-shot. 50 BMG handgun by Triple Action, LLC of Utah. One example made by designer Janos Lakatos was shown at the 2004 SHOT show, where Triple Action looked for manufacturers for production. They did not find any, and Triple Action eventually shut down.

(One example made, shown in 2004)

name: TriStar Cobra Force
description:
The Cobra Force is a Turkish pump-action shotgun manufactured by Armsan and imported into the United States by TriStar Sporting Arms. It's forearm features a return spring that gives the weapon a faster fire rate.

The TriStar Cobra Force and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Feed system: 5 (Some versions have longer tube magazines; but those are modified to hold only 5 rounds due to import restrictions).

name: Tranter Revolver
description:
The Tranter revolver was a double-action cap & ball revolver invented around 1856 by English firearms designer William Tranter (1816–1890). Originally operated with a special dual-trigger mechanism (one to rotate the cylinder and cock the gun, a second to fire it) later models employed a single-trigger mechanism much the same as that found in the contemporary Beaumont-Adams revolver.

Early Tranter revolvers were generally versions of the various Robert Adams-designed revolver models, of which Tranter had produced in excess of 8000 revolvers by 1853. The first model of his own design used the frame of an Adams-type revolver, with a modification in the mechanism which he had jointly developed with James Kerr. The first model was sold under the name Tranter-Adams-Kerr.

name: TRIARC TRI-11
description:
The TRIARC Systems TRI-11 is a 2011-type series of handguns manufactured by TRIARC Systems, mostly known for their high-end AR-15 models. It uses STI /Staccato compatible magazines and is available in numerous finishes, in Government and Commander length and can be optic-ready.

The TRIARC TRI-11 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Trapdoor Springfield Rifle
description:
The Trapdoor Springfield refers to a series of breech loading rifles developed by Springfield, beginning with the Springfield Model 1868 (chambered in. 50-70) up to the Springfield Model 1870 (. 45-70) and then the most famous Model 1873, which was adopted by the U. S. Army (chambered in. 45-70), and the later Models 1877, 1884 and 1888.

The Model 1873 (full sized or cavalry carbine version) is the most likely variant of the Trapdoor Springfield rifle to appear in movies or television shows.

name: Tru Flite 37mm Super Long Range Gas Gun
description:
The Tru Flite 37mm Super Long Range Gas Gun was built at the Lake Erie Chemical Company (Cleveland, Ohio U. S. A. ) in the 1930s-1940s. It was designed for launching 37mm CS gas or Smoke grenades during police standoffs. Distress and illumination flares were also sold for this launcher. The thick solid steel receiver and barrel are able to withstand 'hot' loads, enabling the launcher to propel grenades over longer distances than was usual for its day. If a name eight words in length seems like overkill, this gun's name really is that long and is engraved on the receiver and barrel in its entirety. The reddish color of the receiver is the standard factory finish. Some police departments spray painted their lower receivers black and many examples in collections and museums have black-painted lowers.

The Tru Flite 37mm Super Long Range Gas Gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: TTS Xceed
description:
The TTS Xceed is a bolt-action bullpup sniper rifle manufactured by Tec Target Schneider GmbH since 2021.

The TTS Xceed and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

The Type 1 heavy machine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

*Like the other WW2 Japanese "heavy" machine guns, the Type 1 should really be considered a medium machine gun, as it fires a rifle bullet and does not have any capacity to accept a belt feed; only the weight of the weapon (81 pounds with a tripod) really qualifies it as a "heavy" machine gun.

name: Type 10 naval gun
description:
The 12cm/45 Type 10 was a heavy anti-aircraft gun of the Imperial Japanese Navy, derived from the 12cm/45 3rd Year Type naval gun. It was mounted aboard a number of heavy cruiser classes, but was replaced by the newer 12. 7cm/40 Type 89. It would later see use as a land-based dual-purpose weapon during World War II, as well as aboard some smaller vessels.

The Type 10 naval gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 1 47mm anti-tank gun
description:
The Type 1 47mm anti-tank gun was adopted by the Imperial Japanese Army in 1941. Not to be confused with the Type 1 37mm anti-tank gun, adopted in the same year.

The Type 1 47mm anti-tank gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 01 LMAT
description: The Type 01 LMAT and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Weight: : Complete system: 38. 6 lbs (17. 5 kg), missile: 25. 1 lbs (11. 4 kg)

name: Type 10 signal pistol
description:
The Type 10 was a signal pistol of the Imperial Japanese Army, adopted in 1921 and used through to the end of World War II. Its flares came in black, yellow, white, red, and green. It is visually similar to the Type 26 Revolver (sharing its spurless hammer, trigger guard, and grips), but also evidently different in operation.

The Imperial Japanese Navy would use the Type 90 instead.

name: Type 100 Aircraft Machine Gun
description:
The Type 100 Aircraft Machine Gun is a double barreled aircraft machine gun of Japanese origin. The weapon is gas operated and fed from an overhead magazine.

The Type 100 aircraft machine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 100 grenade launcher
description:
The Japanese Type 100 grenade launcher was introduced in 1939 as a grenade discharger on Arisaka Rifles for Type 99 hand grenades. The launcher is somewhat unusual in that rather than using the more common cup or spigot designs it is a gas trap system, meaning that it incorporates a barrel extension which taps off excess propellent gases to launch the grenade from a cup offset from the barrel. This has the advantage that standard rifle cartridges could be used (along with the standard Type 99 hand grenade) which simplified logistics, at the expense of increased weight and decreased efficiency. The launcher was manufactured from 1942 until 1945, with approximately 36, 000 produced.

The Type 100 grenade launcher has appeared in the following films, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 11 Incendiary/Smoke grenade
description:
The Type 11 it is a Japanese incendiary/smoke grenade from the Second World War.

The Type 11 Incendiary/Smoke grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 2 grenade launcher
description:
The Type 2 rifle grenade launcher was an attachment for the Japanese Type 38 and Type 99 rifles that allowed them to fire special hollow charge grenades. It was a version of the German Schiessbecher grenade launcher. Two grenades were produced for the launcher: a 30 mm calibre grenade and a larger 40 mm calibre grenade, both designated Type 2.

The Type 2 grenade launcher has appeared in the following films, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 11 light machine gun
description:
The Type 11 was a light machine gun used by the Imperial Japanese Army in the interwar period and during World War II. The Type 11 came into active service in 1922, and some 29, 000 were produced by the time production stopped in 1941. It was the primary Japanese light machine gun through the Manchurian Incident and in the early stages of the Second Sino-Japanese War. Although superseded by the Type 96 in production in 1936, it remained in service with front-line combat through the end of World War II.

The Type 11 light machine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 14 Pistol
description:
The Type 14 Pistol was developed in the mid-1920s and officially adopted by the Imperial Japanese Army in 1925. Because it is an improved version of the Nambu Mark B pistol developed in the 1900s, it is often referred to as the Nambu Type 14 Pistol, but its official name is the Type 14 Pistol, and Nambu Kijiro was not directly involved in its design. The actual designer is believed to have been Captain Tomonori Yoshida of the Tokyo Arsenal of the Imperial Japanese Army.

In 1924 (Taisho 13), the large gun (Type B) was adopted by the Naval Landing Corps and approximately 10, 000 were delivered under the designation Riku-shiki gun. In 1925 (Taisho 14), the army produced a prototype of an improved version of the large gun (Type B) at the Nagoya Arsenal, which was adopted as the Type 14 gun.

name: Type 3 anti-tank/ anti-personnel mine
description:
The Type 3 mine is a Japanese World War II anti-tank/ anti-personnel mine.

The Type 3 anti-tank/ anti-personnel mine and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 26 Revolver
description:
The Type 26 was an Imperial Japanese Revolver, first adopted in 1893 and used until the end of the Second World War. It was chambered in 9x22mmR Japanese Revolver, which was a proprietary cartridge similar to the. 38 S&W. It is a top break revolver based on a Smith & Wesson design; notably, it has a bobbed hammer, and is thus a double-action-only revolver.

The Japanese Type 26 Revolver and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 3 heavy machine gun
description:
A license-produced derivative of the Hotchkiss M1914 Machine Gun. Smaller cousin of the Type 92 heavy machine gun.

Fire Modes: Fully Automatic (450 rounds per minute)

name: Type 5 cannon
description:
The Type 5 cannons is a 30mm autocannon developed by and used on some Imperial Japanese Navy combat aircraft during later stages of World War II.

The Type 5 cannon and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Action: Gas-unlocked blowback with floating firing, Kjellman locked.

name: Type 4 Rocket Launcher
description:
The Type 4 Rocket Launcher is a Japanese rocket launcher used during the last years of World War II. It was to be used in the Japanese mainland in case of an invasion by the Allies. It was comparable to the German Panzerschreck and the American Bazooka. Originally, the weapon was designed for 70mm ammunition. A launcher for 90mm ammunition was later designed, but 90mm ammunition never went into production.

The Type 4 Rocket Launcher and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 5 Recoilless Rifle
description: The Type 5 Recoilless Rifle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 63 rifle
description:
The Type 63 rifle, also incorrectly referred as the Type 68 by Western sources (referencing its date of adoption), is a Chinese made assault rifle. It was made to replace the Type 56 in Chinese service when soldiers needed a combination of long rifle range and rifle firepower. Despite resembling the SKS, it uses the rotating bolt system of the AK-47 rather than the tilting bolt system of the SKS. The rifle was manufactured by Factory 296, now known as Chongqing Jianshe.

A folding-stock variant was also made in limited numbers for paratroopers. It was also exported to Vietnam, Cambodia, North Korea, Anti-Soviet forces in Afghanistan, and Albania. Semi-auto surplus variants were also exported to Australia before the introduction of the 1996 restrictions on the legal ownership of semi-automatic longarms and pump-action shotguns.

specs.Feed System: 20-round box magazine, AK magazines (requires removal of bolt-hold open device)

name: Type 53 signal pistol
description: The Type 53 signal pistol and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 64 pistol
description:
The Type 64 (Chinese: 64式手枪; Literally "Type 64 Handgun") was the first indigenous pistol design developed by the People's Republic of China. Based on the Type 52 ( Walther PPK ) and Type 59 ( Makarov PM ) pistols, the Type 64 pistol uses the Walther PPK's straight blowback, open bolt single-action system while incorporating the Makarov PM's safety system. The Type 64 is chambered in the unique 7. 62x17mm round, which is similar in dimensions to the. 32 ACP.

The Type 64 was designed between 1960 and 1964, but was not introduced into service until 1980. Initially issued to high-ranking People's Liberation Army officers and officials, the Type 64 has since been replaced in PLA service by the QSZ-92. However, it remains in use with the People's Armed Police and some civilian police forces alongside the ZLS-05 revolver. The Type 64 is still use with the plainclothes police in China for now.

name: Type 67 machine gun
description:
The Type 67 is a Chinese general-purpose machine gun, and China's first domestic machine gun design that is not a copy of preexisting foreign designs.

Like other Chinese small arms, it is commonly misreported as being designed and manufactured by Norinco Group by internet sources; the machine gun was actually designed and manufactured by Factory 356, now known as Yunnan Xiyi Industrial Co Ltd, a subsidiary of China South Industries Group.

name: Type 64 submachine gun
description:
The Type 64 (Chinese: 1964年式衝鋒槍; pinyin: 1964 Niánshì Chōngfēng Qiāng; '1964 Year Type Submachine Gun') is a Chinese submachine gun with an integral suppressor that use 7. 62×25mm Tokarev ammo. It was first used by the NVA in the Vietnam War.

The Type 64 submachine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 64 silenced pistol
description:
The Type 64 pistol is an integrally suppressed Chinese handgun for covert military usage. It is chambered for special ball 7. 65mm Type 64 ammunition. Due to its low muzzle velocity, the Type 64's report is intended to be inaudible at 10-20 meters. It was introduced into service in the mid-1960s, and was developed into the more refined Type 67 silenced pistol.

The Type 64 silenced pistol and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 56 assault rifle
description:
The Type 56 assault rifle is the Chinese clone of the Soviet AK-47 rifle. It was China's secondary service rifle, while the Type 56 SKS was their mainstay service rifle. It was in service from 1956 to the adoption of the QBZ-95 in around 1997, and was designated as a submachine gun under the PLA due to their doctrine. It is the most produced variant of the AK rifle in the world.

The Type 56 is the most commonly used AK variant in American film and television productions. This came about because China exported civilian AK variants (both Type 56 patterened and less commonly Soviet AK patterened) to the West in large numbers during the 1980s, primarily by the export companies Norinco (not to be confused with Norinco Group ) and Poly Technologies, however they were made China Ordnance Industries Group Corporation Limited (which is just the umbrella company for Norinco). Most American movie prop rental houses had dozens if not hundreds of Type 56s, usually semi-automatic civilian versions, in many cases converted to full-auto by Class III manufacturers. They were imported with the names "AK-47 Sporter", "AKS-47", "Type 56 Sporter", "AK Sporter", etc. and stamped with the name 'Norinco" as the manufacturer on the receivers. There were far more Chinese AKs with the name "AKS-47" or "AK-47 Sporter" in the US than "Type 56" (a decision due to the American Public not being familiar with the name "Type 56").

specs.Capacity: 30-round box magazine, 40-rounds box magazines, AK-47 type magazines (7. 62x39mm)

name: Type 72 Anti-Personnel Mine
description: The Type 72 Anti-Personnel Mine and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 67 stick grenade
description:
Type 67 stick grenade is a Chinese hand grenade based on German Model 24 Stielhandgranate. This grenade was widely used during the Vietnam War where it was known as "Chinese Type II".

The Type 67 stick grenade can be seen in the following films and video games used by the following actors:

name: Type 73 Light Machine Gun
description:
The Type 73 is a light machine gun, designed and manufactured by North Korea's First Machine Industry Bureau for use by the Korean People's Army. The machine gun is mostly based on the Soviet PK but incorporates dual magazine/belt feed system, patterned after the Czechoslovak Vz. 52. The feature, most unusual for machine guns, is a barrel attachment for firing rifle grenades.

The Type 73 is main squad automatic weapon of the Korean People's Army. It was also supplied to Iran and to various non-government powers on Middle East.

name: Type 730 CIWS
description: The Type 730 is a Chinese seven-barrelled, 30mm rotary cannon. A land based configuration called LD2000 (LuDun-2000) has it mounted on the WS-2400's chassis. The navy designation is H/PJ-12. The eleven barrel Type 1130 appears to be based on it. A land based configuration called LD3000 (LuDun-3000) is mounted on a trailer and towed via a 6x6 truck. The navy designation is H/PJ11.

name: Type 79 Jyu-MAT
description: The Type 79 Jyu-MAT and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 77
description:
The Type 77 is a Chinese semi-automatic pistol in service with all branches of the People's Liberation Army (PLA) of the People's Republic of China, People's Armed Police and various Chinese police forces. The Type 77 has been the main sidearm of the PLA for over two decades and is slowly being phased out by the QSZ-92 pistol in both military and police service but is still in widespread use across the country amongst second line military units and provincial police forces.

Beginning in 1990, several variants of the Type 77 were developed for the international market. Most versions feature an unusual one-hand cocking system in which pulling back the front of the trigger guard with the index finger will rack the slide. The Type 77 was exported by the Norinco export company (not to be confused with the manufacturer, Norinco Group ), and export models featured Norinco markings.

name: Type 79 submachine gun
description:
The Type 79 is a Chinese submachine gun. The weapon was designed from 1970 to 1979 by No. 208 Research Institute, today a subsidiary of China South Industries Group, based on an even earlier submachine gun design developed from 1965-1969. They appear to have been primarily manufactured by China's Factory 346, though a "Shanghai-made" clone (exact manufacturer unknown) also exists.

The Type 79 submachine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 81
description:
The Type 81 is a family of Chinese assault rifle and light machine gun designs developed in the late 1970s and finalized in 1981. Despite common misconception as being manufactured by Norinco, the family of weapons was actually manufactured by Factory 296, now known as Jianshe (a subsidiary of the China South Industries Group ); however, small numbers of Type 81s were exported to the west by Norinco (not to be confused with Norinco Group), under their Norinco branding.

The fixed-stocked rifle variant of the Type 81 family.

name: Type 86P Hand Grenade
description:
The Type 86P is a high-explosive fragmentation (HE-Frag), anti-personnel (AP) and defensive grenade. The HE-Frag hand grenade contains approximately 1, 600 steel pellets (2. 5–3 mm) embedded in plastic. A defensive fragmentation hand grenade consisting of an oval main body and a fuse. The Type 86P's main body is made of olive green plastic and features a series of horizontal and vertical external ribs for easy grip. At the top of the main body is a thin-walled, cylindrical neck with an external thread, to which the fuse is attached.

The Type 86P Hand Grenade and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 82-2
description:
The Type 82-2 is a Chinese survival knife with. 22 LR firing mechanisms integrated into the handle. The weapon was developed by the East China Institute of Engineering (now known as Nanjing University of Science and Technology) and Factory 972 (now known as Zhejiang Xinhua Machinery Manufacturing Co. , Ltd. )

The Type 82-2 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Fire Modes: Safe / fire selector on knife grip

name: Type 85 heavy machine gun
description:
The Type 85 is a Chinese heavy machine gun. It is a second-generation Chinese indigenous HMG derived from the Type 77 HMG. It is not to be confused with the W-85 heavy machine gun.

Its export variant is named W-95 (W standing for 外贸, wài mào, "export"), and is chambered in. 50 BMG. The different chambering necessitates the redesign of some parts of the weapon.

specs.Weight: 18. 3 kg (empty gun), 17. 1 kg (tripod), 40 kg (total weight including gun, tripod, empty belt box, telescopic sight, gun stock)

name: Type 82 hand grenade
description: The Type 82 hand grenade appears in the following video games:

name: Type 87 Chu-MAT
description:
The Type 87 Chu-MAT is a Japanese SACLOS guided crew-served heavy anti-tank missile launcher developed in the 1970s alongside the smaller Type 79 Jyu-MAT as a replacement for the obsolete MCLOS Type 64 MAT missile system. Unusually for a ground-mounted ATGM system, the Chu-MAT uses SALH (semi-active laser homing) as its guidance method, with the missile seeking reflected light from an emitter which can either be mounted on the launcher tripod or on its own tripod up to 220 yards (200m) from the launcher.

The Type 87 Chu-MAT and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Fire Modes: Single shot, semi-active laser homing (SALH) guidence

name: Type 85 submachine gun
description:
The Type 85 is a family of Chinese submachine guns (including suppressed and unsuppressed variants) formalized in 1985. The weapon was developed to replace the Type 64 submachine gun and the Type 79 submachine gun. It was developed and manufactured by Factory 9616 (now known as Hubei Jianghua Machinery Co Ltd), and was trialed alongside a number of other Chinese submachine gun designs.

The Type 85 submachine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 86S
description:
The Type 86S (Chinese: 86S式自动步枪 / 86S shì zìdòng bùqiāng / "Type 86S automatic rifle") is a Chinese semi-automatic bullpup rifle based on the AK-47. The rifle was exported by Norinco and was branded as a Norinco weapon, though its actual designers and manufacturers are unknown (Norinco themselves do not have manufacturing capabilities).

The Type 86S was rather clunky and did not exhibit fine fit or finish. It was exported for civilian use and it was sold worldwide in hopes of cashing in on the 'bullpup market' that many expected to explode in the 1980s, however despite offerings like the Steyr AUG, bullpup sales never took off.

specs.Feed System: 7. 62 AK/Type 56 magazines, supplied and usually used with 20-round magazines as they are easier to load into the gun

name: Type 88 75 mm AA gun
description:
Type 88 75 mm AA gun was an anti-aircraft gun used by the Imperial Japanese Army during the Second Sino-Japanese War and World War II.

The Type 88 75 mm AA gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Mass: 2, 450 kg (5, 401 lb) Firing, 2, 740 kg (6, 041 lb) Traveling
specs.Length: 5 m (16 ft 5 in) Firing, 4 m (13 ft 1 in) Traveling
specs.Width: 1. 6 m (5 ft 3 in) Track, 1. 93 m (6 ft 4 in) Maximum firing

name: Type 91 grenade launcher
description:
The Type 91 grenade launcher is a series of Chinese underbarrel grenade launchers. The variants in the series are:

Its design is similar to the M203 grenade launcher. While it could theoretically fire lethal grenades, currently only less-lethal grenades are reported to exist for the weapon.

name: Type 91 MANPADS
description:
The Type 91 MANPADS is a Japanese man-portable surface-to-air missile system developed in the 80s due to Japanese dissatisfaction with early FIM-92 Stinger variants. The project began in 1982 using a seeker design which had been in development since 1979: for perspective, this was when the Stinger was still the initial, easily spoofed IR-only FIM-92A model.

While it is sometimes dismissed as a straight copy of Stinger and it is certainly heavily based on it, the Type 91 is in several ways a more advanced system, in particular in that it uses imaging infrared instead of contrast infrared: like all Stingers starting with the FIM-92B, it combines this with a UV seeker to defeat countermeasures.

specs.Years of Use: Original - Developed 1982-1990, adopted 1991, first use 1994: Kai Upgrade - 2007-Present

name: Type 90 signal pistol
description:
The Type 90 is a signal pistol of Japanese origin that was used by the Imperial Japanese Navy during WW2. The designation is from the last two digits of its year of adoption, which was 2590 (or 1930 AD) on the Japanese Kōki calendar. It first came with three barrels with just less than 6, 000 were manufactured, but eventually manufactured with two barrels for ease of production late in World War 2. Several variations of the Type 90 were produced. The easiest way to identify the variant of the Type 90 is by the shape of the break-open lever under the barrels. The first variation has a curved vertical lever, the second variation has a straight vertical lever, and the third variation has a curved horizontal lever. Coming in either double or triple barrels, the Type 90 is often seen in many movies wrongly portrayed often as a shotgun pistol due to its unusual appearance. The flares came in white, red, yellow, green, blue, and black. They had cardboard hulls with a brass or steel base.

The Imperial Japanese Army instead used the Type 10.

name: Type 89 "Knee Mortar"
description:
The Type 89 "Knee Mortar" (jap. 八九式重擲弾筒 Hachikyū-shiki jū-tekidantō "Type 89 Heavy Grenade Launcher") or more formally the Type 89 Grenade Discharger was a rifled infantry light mortar used by Japanese forces during World War II: though referred to as a grenade launcher by the Japanese, it does not really fit the modern definition of such. The weapon's nickname comes from the phrase "Leg Mortar" being associated with it in Japanese, referring to how it was normally carried in a leg bag, but this was mistranslated to "Knee Mortar. " This is said to have resulted in some soldiers trying out captured ones by bracing them against their thigh, imagining the curved baseplate was intended to help with this. Actually doing so could result in severe bruising at best and broken bones at worst. The degree to which this occurred is highly exaggerated: any GI who had fired a rifle grenade would understand that firing such a device rested on their thigh was a bad idea.

The mortar was an unconventional design, conceptually influenced by WW1-era light "trench mortars. " It resembled a rifle grenade launching cup attached to a short length of "barrel" (which actually contained the firing pin and trigger assembly), attached to a small curved rectangular baseplate. It was rifled and fired using a trigger, both unusual features for a mortar. The mortar was designed for a fixed firing angle of 45 degrees rather than being aimed by elevating the barrel: a spirit level bubble was clamped near the base of the barrel on the right side to ensure the weapon was at the correct firing angle. The range was instead set using an elevating knob on the side of the weapon, which had calibrated scale for both types of round it could fire. Turning the knob moved the trigger and firing pin assembly inside the gun, in turn altering the distance the projectile was allowed to go inside the barrel and so the distance it was under the influence of pressure from the propellant charge before leaving the barrel. Muzzle-loading the rifled mortar was accomplished with a copper driving band around the propellant charge holder of the projectile: this was sub-caliber until the propelling charge detonated: vents in the sides of the propellant base under the copper band then forced it to expand to fit the rifling grooves. The rifling does not seem to have been a factor when not firing the weapon's purpose-built shells. A flexible sheath was provided at the base of the lower part of the weapon, the top of which could be pulled upwards to cover the lower part of the slit the trigger group moved in to prevent dirt or other material from entering the weapon.

name: Type 92 heavy machine gun
description:
The Type 92 heavy machine gun was essentially a Type 3 heavy machine gun chambered for the larger 7. 7x58mm SR round; as, with its cousin, it is a derivative of the Hotchkiss M1914 Machine Gun. Due to its relatively slow rate of fire, the allies nicknamed it "the woodpecker" while Chinese soldiers called it the "chicken neck" due to its appearance. The gun was almost always employed from a special heavyweight tripod which included holes on the front legs, allowing rods to be passed through so the gun crew could move the weapon by carrying it like a stretcher. Notably it was one of the few WW2 machine guns commonly issued with optics, with several sights being designed specifically for it.

While it was extremely accurate (only weighing five pounds less than a tripod-mounted Browning M2 while firing a rifle cartridge will do that) this was to the point that it was detrimental to its function as a suppression weapon, and it depended on an obsolete loading method with 30-round feed strips that had to be inserted extremely carefully to avoid misfeeds: just to make matters worse, the Japanese were not done with obsolete features and incorporated an integral oiler into the design to ensure reliability, which in the jungles of the Pacific theatre ensured quite the opposite. It also used specially-made ammunition which did not help with logistics at all (Imperial Japan used a total of four non-interchangeable 7. 7x58mm Arisaka cartridges): rifle ammo would physically fit in the Type 92 but was unable to cycle it.

name: Type 89 naval gun
description:
The 12. 7cm/40 Type 89 was a Japanese heavy anti-aircraft gun introduced before World War II. Well-regarded as an anti-air weapon, it replaced the 12cm/45 Type 10 as the standard heavy AA gun aboard cruisers, battleships, and carriers, as well as serving as the primary armament of the Matsu -class destroyers. The vast majority of Type 89s were used twin mounts, though the Matsu -class featured a forward single-mount and rear twin. Some Type 89s were also used on land, both twins and singles.

The Type 89 naval gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Maximum firing range: 9, 440 meters (30, 970 ft) at 90° (AA ceiling), 14, 800 meters (48, 600 ft) at 45°

name: Type 93 heavy machine gun
description:
The Type 93 heavy machine gun is the Imperial Japanese Navy's imported and domestically produced version of the Hotchkiss M1929. The Hotchkiss was also used by the Imperial Japanese Army under the name Type Ho 13mm AA machine cannon ("Ho" taken from "Hotchkiss"). The Army's Type Ho was used as a stationary heavy machine gun and as an anti-aircraft weapon, while the Navy's Type 93 was primarily mounted aboard ships. Being developed before Hotchkiss switched the French model from 13. 2x99mm to 13. 2x96mm in 1935, the Japanese guns continued to use the original cartridge. The IJN's later Type 3 aircraft machine gun would, however, use the newer 13. 2x96mm Hotchkiss cartridge.

The Hotchkiss design would also be the basis for the Type 98 cannon.

name: Type 93 / 100 Flamethrower
description:
The Type 93 and Type 100 flamethrowers were the primary flamethrowers used by the Imperial Japanese Army during the Second World War.

Type 93 Flamethrower (1933 – 1940)

name: Type 96 cannon
description:
The Type 96 was a 25mm machine cannon, which was used by the Imperial Japanese Navy in World War II as an anti-aircraft gun. The designation Type 96 means the year of the first development, the year Kōki 2596 or 1936 according to the Gregorian calendar. The weapon was an air-cooled gas operated loader, which shot with a theoretical firing sequence of 220 rounds per minute 25mm bullets with a muzzle velocity of 900 meters per second.

After various problems with the water-cooled 40mm Vickers QF 2-pounder (or Type Bi in Japanese service) became known, the Japanese Navy decided in 1935 to replace them with a gun based on the French 25mm Hotchkiss AA gun. After studies on the weapon in France, some patterns were ordered with changes according to Japanese wishes at Hotchkiss. The weapon now reached with a 250g heavy bullet a theoretical rate of fire of 180 to 200 rounds per minute. The Japanese made further changes and exchanged the muzzle-fire damper for a model based on a development by Rheinmetall and, to a lesser extent, the function of a muzzle brake. Furthermore, manufacturing was simplified by the use of castings in areas where previously forged parts had been used. The mass production of the weapon began after the trials in 1936 and the Type 96 was gradually introduced on all warships of the Imperial Japanese Navy as a standard weapon for air defense at close range. The weapon was mounted singly (1943), in groups of two (1936, first variant) or three (1941) on gun carriages.

name: Type 97 hand grenade
description:
The Type 97 hand grenade was the main grenade used by Japanese forces during the Second World War. It was more or less just the earlier multi-purpose Type 91 grenade with the attachment socket for a propellant canister deleted from the base, preventing it from being used as a rifle grenade or light mortar projectile as the Type 91 could be, and with the 7-8 second fuze replaced with a 4-5 second fuze to prevent the grenade being thrown back.

Many Type 91s were retrofitted with this fuze: they were further modified by having their base threading drilled out, and had blobs of welding flux added to the lip at the top of the base so they would not physically fit inside a mortar or rifle grenade launcher. For identification, the bases of these grenades were painted white: any grenade that appears to be a Type 97 but has a white base with a hollow in it is actually a modified Type 91. Without a white base but with a visible recessed socket, it is a regular Type 91. With a white base and no hollow, it is an error, as no grenades were marked in this way.

name: Type 93 Landmine
description:
The Type 93 landmine was adopted by the Japanese Imperial Army in 1933. It was used during the Battle of Khalkhin Gol in 1939, and throughout the rest of WWII, notably on Iwo Jima. The mine was classified as anti-tank but the variable operating pressure of the fuze allowed it to also be used as anti-personnel. The body of the mine was made of copper or brass that hampered the usage of metal detectors.

The Type 93 Landmine and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 97 Infantry Mortar
description:
Type 97 Infantry Mortar is a Japanese 81-mm (also in version 90-mm) mortar used primary by Imperial Japanese Army during World War II. It entered service in 1937.

Weight: 65 kilograms (145 lb ), 105 kg (233 lb)

name: Type 97 flare pistol
description:
The Type 97 flare pistol was a Japanese flare gun used during World War II.

The Type 97 flare pistol and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 97 tank machine gun
description:
The Type 97 tank machine gun was the standard machine gun used in tanks and armored vehicles of the Imperial Japanese Army during World War II, a heavy machine gun by infantry forces. This weapon was not related to the Type 97 aircraft machine gun used in many Japanese Navy aircraft, including the Mitsubushi A6M Zero.

The Type 97 tank machine gun and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 94 Smoke Candle
description:
The Type 94 it is a Japanese smoke candle from the Second World War period.

The Type 94 Smoke Candle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 97 anti-tank rifle
description:
Developed from the Type 98 cannon, the Type 97 automatic cannon (九七式自動砲, Kyū-nana-shiki-jidōhō) is a semi-automatic 20mm Japanese anti-tank rifle. It would later be developed into two aircraft cannons, the Ho-1 for flexible mounts and the Ho-3 (with a drum magazine) for fixed mounts.

The Type 97 anti-tank rifle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 99 81 mm Mortar
description:
Type 99 81 mm Mortar is a Japanese 81 mm mortar.

The Type 99 81 mm Mortar and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type Kō rifle
description:
The Type Kō ("kō" or "kou" being equivalent to "A") was a Japanese prototype self-loading rifle. The Type Kō rifle utilized a copy of the Pedersen Rifle action. It was tested alongside the Type Otsu and Type Hei.

The Type Kō rifle and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

specs.Length: 45. 7 in (116 cm) - Long / 41. 7 in (106 cm) - Short
specs.Barrel length: 26. 5 in (67. 2 cm) - Long / 22. 6 in (57. 3 cm) - Short

name: Type Hei rifle
description:
The Type Hei ( Hei being the Japanese character for C) is a prototype semi-automatic Japanese rifle. It was one of three experimental semi-automatic rifles commissioned for testing by the Imperial Japanese Army in 1932 and produced by Nippon Special Steel; the other two were the Type Kō and Type Otsu. It utilizes a toggle-lock action similar to the action seen on the experimental Pedersen Rifle. The rifle was offered with 5-round or 10-round magazine capacities, and could be loaded using Arisaka stripper clips. The Type Hei along with several other semi-automatic rifles submitted for evaluation by the Japanese military were tested in 1935, but none were found suitable for mass production and use.

The Type Hei and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 98 cannon
description:
The Type 98 was the standard light anti-air weapon of the Imperial Japanese Army during the World War II era, developed from the Hotchkiss M1929, which itself was used by the IJA as the Type Ho. The Type 98 would serve as the basis for the Type 97 anti-tank rifle and its derivatives the Ho-1 and Ho-3.

The Type 98 cannon and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type 99 hand grenade
description:
The Type 99 hand grenade was introduced in 1939 after observations of behaviors of the Type 91 and Type 97 grenades in the colder regions of Northern China and in Manchuria. It consisted of a cast iron body without ribbing. The bottom was now thinner so that despite lower dimensions, the weight of the explosive charge was equal to the type 97 grenade. However, because of the use of picric acid instead of TNT, the explosive effect was significantly greater. Since the picric acid attacked the steel, the interior of the shell was coated with a protective varnish.

The normal impact fuse had been modified to produce fewer duds under the more extreme weather conditions. So the gas outlet was now covered with a closed sheet metal tray. In this tray, holes were drilled from above at an angle of 90 ° to the two gas outlets. So it could be prevented that the escaping hot gas jet burned the launcher. In addition, it was prevented so that the glowing gas jet could be seen by the enemy at night.

name: Type 99 cannon
description:
Not to be confused with the Type 99 light machine gun.

The Type 99 cannons are a pair of related Japanese aircraft cannons derived from the Oerlikon FF series; the Type 99 Mark 1 and Type 99 Mark 2 were developed from the smaller FF F and the mid-size FF L, respectively. They were the standard 20mm cannon of the Imperial Japanese Navy before and during World War II, mounted on many of their aircraft.

name: Typhoon F12
description: The Typhoon F12 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Type Otsu rifle
description:
The Type Otsu ("otsu" being equivalent to "B") is a Japanese prototype self-loading rifle, a copy of the Czechoslovakian ZH-29. It was tested alongside the Type Kō and Type Hei.

(Design from 1931 to 1935. Field trials in 1935 - prototype only)

specs.Years of Use: Design from 1931 to 1935. Field trials in 1935 - prototype only
specs.Capacity: 5-round internal magazine. Loaded with clips from Arisaka Rifle.

name: UC-9
description:
The UC-9 (Under Cover 9mm), later known as the DEB M21 (David E. Boatman Model 21), was a submachine gun designed by American Utah Connor and produced by David Boatman. It is a folding submachine gun, in the same vein as the Ares FMG, though the two were developed independently and had no relations beyond conceptual similarities.

The first prototype was developed by Utah Connor as the UC-9. Connor was later contacted by Tim Bixler for an opportunity to produce more of the weapon. Bixler contacted gun maker David E. Boatman to produce the later guns. The three improved on the prototype design and designated the produced weapons as the DEB M21. Nine M21s were produced in total, each having a different colored finish aside from two weapons in black. The Hughes Amendment came into effect in 1986 and ended the rest of the production.

specs.Capacity: Compatible with 9mm Uzi magazines; can only fold up with Uzi magazines that are 32 rounds or smaller

name: Unique Model D
description:
Unique Model D was a French small-caliber target pistol, manufactured by Manufacture d' Armes des Pyrénées Françaises (MAPF). It was introduced in 1954, and produced in several versions, from D1 to D6, that differed in barrel length (from 4. 25" to 8. 5"). The production continued until an as-of-yet unknown point in the 21st century (some sources claim 2004).

The Unique Model D and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: UK vz. 59
description:
The UK vz. 59 (CZECH: U niverzální K ulomet v zor 19 59 | ENG: Universal Machine Gun Model 1959) is a general-purpose machine gun developed in Czechoslovakia.

The UK vz. 59 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Unique Alpine TPG-1
description:
The Unique Alpine TPG-1 ( T aktisches P räzisions G ewehr - 1, German for tactical precision rifle ) is manufactured in Austria by local Unique Alpine AG. It was designed as a modular, high precision rifle for special military and law enforcement applications.

The Unique Alpine TPG-1 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Unique Model L
description:
The Unique L was manufactured by French company Manufacture d' Armes des Pyrénées Françaises (MAPF). The Unique Model L Pistol was introduced in the mid-1960s and was an updated version of the various pre-war models. It was a simple, well-made design and equipped some regional French police detectives into the 1980s.

The Unique Model L and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Unique Bcf-66
description:
Unique Bcf-66 (sometimes mentioned as BCF 66 ) is a French pistol that was manufactured by Manufacture d' Armes des Pyrénées Françaises (MAPF) in 1954-1980. Its design with open-topped slide is based on pre-war Beretta models.

The Unique Bcf-66 and variants can be seen in the following films, television series, video games, and anime used by the following actors:

name: Ultimax 100
description:
The Ultimax 100 was developed by Chartered Industries of Singapore (CIS) in the late 1970s as a indigenous Light Machine Gun for the Singapore Armed Forces. CIS was eventually merged into Singapore Technologies Kinetics (STK). There are seven models of the Ultimax 100:

The Ultimax 100 Light Machine Gun has appeared in the following films used by the following actors:

specs.Barrel Length: 20 in (50. 8 cm): Standard, 13 in (33 cm): Para
